clues: The dashboard page is slow, but each individual query runs in under 10ms
Database connection pool keeps getting exhausted during list page loads
The slow endpoint issues hundreds of nearly identical SQL statements per request
Adding eager loading to the ORM relationship reduced query count from 201 to 2
Each item in the parent collection triggers a separate SELECT to fetch its children
The ORM lazily loads related records one row at a time inside a loop over the parent result set
explanation: A performance anti-pattern where fetching a list of N items triggers N additional queries to load related data, instead of fetching everything in a single joined query.

clues: Users are reporting inconsistent data depending on which server handles their request
A network partition occurred and the system kept accepting writes on both sides
Two nodes each believe they are the authoritative primary and are accepting conflicting writes
After the network healed, we discovered divergent data that cannot be automatically reconciled
The cluster fencing mechanism failed, so both partitions promoted themselves to leader
Two nodes in the same cluster each think they are the sole leader after a network partition, causing divergent writes
explanation: A distributed system failure where a network partition causes two or more nodes to independently assume the leader role, leading to conflicting writes and data divergence.

clues: The service crashes every time it restarts, even though load was fine before the outage
Our cache node went down and the database was immediately overwhelmed
Thousands of clients simultaneously retried when the service came back online, crashing it again
Adding jittered backoff to retry logic eliminated the coordinated spike
A single cache expiration caused every concurrent request to simultaneously hit the database for the same key
All waiting clients stampede the backend at once the instant a shared resource becomes available or expires
explanation: A pattern where a large number of clients simultaneously rush to access a resource when it becomes available or a cache expires, overwhelming the backend.

clues: Every new feature seems to require changes to the same file
Merge conflicts happen constantly because every developer touches this one class
The class has over 5,000 lines and imports from nearly every module in the system
It handles user authentication, order processing, email sending, and report generation all in one place
This single class knows about and orchestrates every subsystem, violating the Single Responsibility Principle
One monolithic class has accumulated so many responsibilities that it effectively controls the entire application's behavior
explanation: An anti-pattern where a single class takes on too many responsibilities, becoming a central point that knows too much and does too much, making the system rigid and hard to maintain.

clues: Deploying to staging requires changing source files and rebuilding the artifact
The database connection string is different on every developer's machine, causing constant local config edits
We can't promote the same build artifact from staging to production without a code change
API keys and service URLs are hardcoded as string literals scattered across the codebase
The app reads its configuration from constants in source files rather than from environment variables
Environment-specific settings like database URLs and API keys are committed directly in source code instead of being injected via environment variables at runtime
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor app principle that configuration varying between deploys should be stored in environment variables, not hardcoded in the application source.

clues: An unrelated microservice went down and somehow our entire platform followed
The initial failure was minor, but within minutes every dependent service was returning errors
Each service that failed increased load on the remaining healthy instances, causing them to fail too
The overloaded authentication service caused the API gateway to queue up, which timed out the frontend, which retried aggressively
Failure propagated through the dependency chain: Service A overwhelmed Service B, which overwhelmed Service C
A single component failure triggered a domino effect where each downstream dependency collapsed in sequence under redistributed load
explanation: A failure mode where the failure of one component causes increasing load or errors on other components, leading to a progressive chain of failures across the system.

clues: Simple feature requests take weeks because so many files need to change
A seemingly trivial change to the pricing logic required modifying 15 different classes
Every time we update the user model, we have to grep the entire codebase for places that reference it
The same type of change is scattered across dozens of files instead of being localized in one module
Adding a single field to the domain object required edits in the controller, three services, two serializers, and four tests
A single logical change forces many small edits scattered across numerous classes because the responsibility is not cohesive
explanation: A code smell where a single change requires making many small modifications to many different classes, indicating that a single responsibility is spread across the codebase.

clues: Users occasionally see stale data right after making updates
A customer updates their profile but the change doesn't appear when they immediately refresh
The read replicas are several seconds behind the primary during peak write load
Read-after-write consistency breaks because the read hits a follower that hasn't caught up to the leader yet
The streaming replication position on the replica is minutes behind the WAL position on the primary
Asynchronous replication from leader to follower introduces a delay where reads from the follower return data that is seconds or minutes stale
explanation: The delay between a write being applied on the primary database and that same write being reflected on read replicas, causing temporary inconsistency.

clues: The on-call engineer spends most of their shift doing the same repetitive tasks
This manual work grows linearly with service size and doesn't produce lasting value
The team spends 60% of their time on operational tasks that could be automated
Every weekly release requires an engineer to manually run the same 20-step deployment checklist
These repetitive, automatable, reactive operational tasks provide no enduring value and scale linearly with service growth
Manual, repetitive, automatable operational work that scales linearly with service size, as defined by the SRE practice as work that should be systematically eliminated
explanation: Work tied to running a production service that is manual, repetitive, automatable, tactical, and scales linearly with service size, producing no lasting improvement.

clues: This method doesn't seem to belong in the class where it's defined
The function accesses more data from another object than from its own
It calls six getters on the Order object but only uses one field from its own class
The calculateShipping method in the InvoiceService class uses five fields from the Order class and none from InvoiceService
This method is more interested in the data of another class than its own, suggesting it should be moved
A method that extensively uses fields and methods of another class rather than its own, indicating it belongs on the other class
explanation: A code smell where a method accesses the data of another object more than its own, suggesting the method should be moved to the class whose data it actually uses.

clues: The product team and SRE team are arguing about whether to ship a risky feature or focus on reliability
We need a quantitative way to balance the pace of innovation against system stability
The team is allowed to take reliability risks as long as the service hasn't consumed its quarterly allocation of acceptable downtime
With 99.9% SLO, we have 43.2 minutes of allowed downtime this month, and we've used 20 of them
Once the remaining allowance of unreliability is exhausted, all feature releases are frozen until reliability improves
The quantified amount of acceptable unreliability derived from the SLO, used to govern the trade-off between feature velocity and reliability
explanation: The amount of unreliability a service is allowed to have, calculated as 1 minus the SLO target, used to make objective decisions about the trade-off between reliability and feature development.

clues: The on-call engineer has started ignoring pages because most of them don't require action
Our monitoring system fires hundreds of alerts daily, and the team has become desensitized
A real production outage was missed because the critical page was buried among dozens of noisy false positives
Over 85% of the alerts that fire are acknowledged and immediately closed without any remediation action
The team's MTTA has tripled because engineers assume every new page is another false positive
Excessive non-actionable alerts have desensitized the on-call team to the point where genuine critical alerts are routinely ignored or delayed
explanation: A condition where the volume of non-actionable or low-priority alerts desensitizes operators, causing them to ignore or slowly respond to genuinely critical alerts.

clues: The system performed fine during testing but fell over within hours of a product launch
Nobody anticipated how much traffic the new marketing campaign would drive
The service ran out of CPU and memory headroom because growth projections were never modeled
Our Black Friday traffic was 4x our provisioned capacity because we only planned for average load
There was no forecast model mapping projected user growth to required compute, storage, and network resources
The failure to model and provision infrastructure ahead of anticipated demand growth led to a preventable outage during a predictable traffic spike
explanation: The failure to anticipate and provision sufficient resources for future demand, leading to outages or degraded performance during predictable traffic increases.

clues: During the traffic spike, the system should have rejected some requests instead of trying to serve them all
The service could have stayed up if it deliberately dropped low-priority requests when overloaded
We need a mechanism to prioritize critical traffic and intentionally reject excess requests before the system is overwhelmed
The reverse proxy is configured to return 503 for new requests once in-flight requests exceed a threshold
When the queue depth exceeds the configured limit, the server drops the oldest non-critical requests to protect capacity for high-priority ones
The deliberate rejection of excess or low-priority traffic during overload to preserve the system's ability to serve critical requests
explanation: A reliability technique where a system intentionally drops or rejects lower-priority requests during overload to preserve its ability to serve higher-priority traffic.

clues: The failing service recovered momentarily but immediately crashed again under a new wave of traffic
Client-side retries are making the overload situation exponentially worse
Each failed request triggers three automatic retries, tripling the load on an already struggling backend
Aggressive retry logic with no backoff turned a partial outage into a total one by multiplying the request volume
The combination of immediate retries from thousands of clients amplified the original load by 10x, preventing any recovery
Clients aggressively retrying failed requests without exponential backoff or jitter create an amplification loop that deepens the overload they are trying to recover from
explanation: A failure amplification pattern where clients retrying failed requests without backoff or jitter multiply the load on an already overloaded system, preventing recovery.

clues: The outage lasted two hours before anyone noticed, even though monitoring was supposedly in place
We had metrics for CPU and memory, but the actual failure mode was something we never thought to measure
The disk was filling up on a non-standard partition that wasn't covered by our standard monitoring template
No alert existed for the message queue depth, so we didn't know it was backed up until consumers timed out
Critical business metrics like checkout success rate had no dashboards or alerts, only infrastructure-level metrics were tracked
A gap in observability coverage where a critical failure mode has no corresponding metric, alert, or dashboard, allowing failures to go undetected
explanation: An observability gap where important failure modes, system behaviors, or business metrics lack monitoring coverage, allowing problems to go undetected until they cause visible impact.

clues: Senior engineers on the team are starting to leave, citing unsustainable working conditions
The rotation is too small, and engineers are paged multiple times every night shift
Team morale is low because operational interruptions leave no time for project work during on-call weeks
The same three engineers handle all production incidents because the team hasn't grown with the service's complexity
Engineers are averaging 4 pages per night shift and spending over 50% of their working hours on incident response
Chronic overload of on-call engineers due to high page volume, small rotation size, and insufficient automation, leading to exhaustion and attrition
explanation: The chronic exhaustion and attrition of engineers caused by excessive on-call burden, including high page frequency, small rotation sizes, and lack of automation to reduce operational load.

clues: We want to ship faster but the deployment process itself is the constraint
Only one specific person knows how to do a production release, and they're on vacation
The release process involves 30 manual steps, three approval gates, and takes an entire day
Teams are batching weeks of changes into a single release because deploying is so costly and slow
The manual, error-prone, single-person-dependent release process limits the team to one deploy per week
The release pipeline is a manual, poorly automated process owned by a single person, creating a bottleneck that limits deployment frequency and increases batch size risk
explanation: A situation where the release and deployment process is so manual, complex, or dependent on specific individuals that it becomes the primary constraint on delivery velocity.

clues: When the recommendation engine is down, the entire product page returns a 500 error
A non-critical dependency failure is taking down critical user-facing functionality
The checkout flow fails completely because the analytics service is unreachable, even though analytics is not essential for checkout
The system has no fallback behavior: every dependency is treated as hard, and any failure is a total failure
When the personalization microservice times out, the page should show generic content instead of an error, but it doesn't
The system lacks fallback paths for non-critical dependencies, causing total failure instead of reduced functionality when optional services are unavailable
explanation: The failure to design a system that can continue providing core functionality in a reduced capacity when non-critical dependencies become unavailable.

clues: Engineers are reluctant to report incidents because they fear being punished
The last incident review turned into a finger-pointing session about who made the bad deploy
People are hiding mistakes instead of surfacing them, so the team keeps making the same errors
The postmortem concluded with 'John should have known better' instead of identifying systemic improvements
Root cause analysis focuses on individual human error rather than the system conditions that made the error possible and undetectable
Incident retrospectives focus on assigning personal blame rather than identifying systemic fixes, discouraging transparency and preventing organizational learning
explanation: An organizational anti-pattern where incident reviews focus on blaming individuals rather than identifying systemic improvements, discouraging transparency and preventing learning from failures.

clues: Most of our outages are caused by something someone changed, not by hardware failures
A config push went out to production without review and broke authentication for all users
There's no process to track, review, or roll back changes made to production systems
The engineer pushed a database migration directly to production during peak hours without telling anyone
70% of incidents trace back to changes deployed without peer review, canary testing, or a rollback plan
The absence of structured processes for reviewing, testing, and rolling back production changes causes a disproportionate number of outages to be change-induced
explanation: The lack of structured processes for controlling changes to production systems, resulting in unreviewed, untested changes being a leading cause of outages.

clues: During the last outage, nobody knew who was in charge or what to do first
Five engineers were all debugging different theories with no coordination
There were no runbooks, no defined roles, and the communication channel was a mess of unrelated threads
The incident commander role doesn't exist, so three people simultaneously made conflicting decisions
There is no defined incident response framework: no commander, no communication lead, no predefined runbooks, and no structured escalation path
The absence of defined roles, runbooks, communication protocols, and an incident commander leads to uncoordinated, slow, and error-prone incident response
explanation: The lack of a structured incident management process with defined roles, communication protocols, and runbooks, leading to disorganized and ineffective incident response.

clues: The service is returning errors but there's no logic bug; the code hasn't changed in weeks
Performance degrades gradually over days until the service eventually crashes
The container keeps getting OOM-killed because a memory leak slowly consumes all available RAM
File descriptors hit the OS limit because connections are opened but never closed
The process ran out of available file descriptors, thread pool capacity, or heap memory due to a leak or missing resource limits
A critical system resource such as memory, file descriptors, disk space, or connection pool slots is fully consumed, causing the service to fail or hang
explanation: A failure mode where a finite system resource like memory, file descriptors, disk space, or connections is fully consumed, causing the service to degrade or crash.

clues: The load balancer thinks all instances are healthy, but users are getting errors
The health endpoint returns 200 OK even when the service can't actually serve requests
Our health check only verifies the process is running, not that it can reach its database or process work
The instance passed the TCP liveness check but its connection pool to the database was exhausted
The /health endpoint returns 200 as long as the HTTP server is up, but doesn't verify database connectivity, cache availability, or downstream dependency health
Shallow health checks that only confirm process liveness without validating actual readiness to serve traffic, causing load balancers to route requests to broken instances
explanation: A reliability gap where health check endpoints are too shallow to detect meaningful failures, causing traffic to be routed to instances that are running but unable to serve requests.

clues: When the payment provider is slow, our entire checkout flow becomes slow too
The service keeps sending requests to a known-broken dependency, tying up threads waiting for timeouts
We need a mechanism to stop calling a failing downstream service and fail fast instead
All threads are blocked waiting on a downstream service that's been returning 503s for the past five minutes
There's no mechanism to detect a failing dependency and temporarily short-circuit calls to it with a fast failure response
The absence of a pattern that monitors downstream failures and temporarily stops sending requests to a broken service, instead failing fast and returning a fallback
explanation: The lack of a circuit breaker pattern that detects when a downstream dependency is failing and temporarily stops sending requests to it, instead failing fast to prevent resource exhaustion.

clues: The bad deploy is causing errors, but we can't undo it quickly
We realized the release was broken in production, but there's no automated way to revert to the previous version
The rollback script hasn't been tested in months and failed when we tried to use it during the incident
A database migration in the release was not backward-compatible, so reverting the code didn't fix the problem
The deployment pipeline has no one-click rollback; reverting requires a manual cherry-pick, rebuild, and redeploy process
The inability to quickly and reliably revert a bad deployment due to untested rollback procedures, irreversible migrations, or missing previous artifacts
explanation: The inability to quickly revert a bad deployment due to missing rollback automation, untested procedures, irreversible database migrations, or unavailable previous build artifacts.

clues: Requests are hanging for a very long time before finally returning an error
The client-side timeout is longer than the server-side timeout, causing confusing error behavior
Threads are stuck waiting on a downstream call with a 60-second default timeout instead of a sensible 2-second limit
The HTTP client was configured with the library's default 30-second timeout, which is far too long for a service that should respond in 100ms
Upstream timeouts are shorter than downstream timeouts, causing the upstream to retry while the downstream is still processing the original request
Improperly configured timeouts across the request chain, such as defaults that are too long or mismatched layers, cause thread exhaustion, cascading delays, and duplicate work
explanation: Incorrectly set timeout values across a service chain that lead to resource waste, thread pool exhaustion, or cascading delays because timeouts are too long, too short, or misaligned between layers.

clues: Users are complaining about slowness, but the engineering team says the system is 'up'
We promised 99.9% availability but delivered 99.5% last quarter, and customers are unhappy
The gap between our promised performance and actual performance is eroding customer trust
Our p99 latency target is 200ms but we've been at 800ms for two weeks and no one flagged it
The formal reliability target agreed upon with stakeholders has been breached, but no defined remediation process was triggered
The measured reliability of the service has fallen below the formally agreed-upon target for availability or latency, triggering the need for corrective action
explanation: When a service's measured reliability or performance falls below the formally defined target agreed upon with stakeholders, indicating that corrective action is needed.

clues: Response times are climbing and error rates are spiking, but no new code was deployed
The CPU is pegged at 100% and the request queue is growing without bound
The service is receiving more traffic than it can handle, and it has no mechanism to reject excess load
Latency has gone from 50ms to 5 seconds because the service has no admission control and is trying to serve every request
The service has no rate limiting, no load shedding, and no auto-scaling, so it degrades badly under traffic spikes
A service receiving more requests than its capacity can handle, with no backpressure or admission control mechanisms, causing latency to spike and errors to cascade
explanation: A service that is receiving more traffic than it can handle without mechanisms like rate limiting, load shedding, or auto-scaling to manage the excess, causing severe degradation.

clues: The team knows what needs to be done during incidents, but it takes too long to do it manually
Operational tasks that should take seconds are taking hours because they require human intervention
Scaling up requires an engineer to manually provision VMs, update configs, and restart services
The disaster recovery plan requires 47 manual steps, and during the last drill it took 6 hours instead of the target 30 minutes
Routine operational procedures like scaling, failover, and certificate rotation are documented in runbooks but not automated
Critical operational procedures remain manual despite being well-understood, repetitive, and automatable, increasing MTTR and operational burden
explanation: The gap between operational tasks that could be automated and those that are actually automated, increasing time-to-resolution and operational burden unnecessarily.

clues: Reading this function requires scrolling through multiple screens
The method has six levels of nested conditionals and handles multiple unrelated concerns
It's nearly impossible to write a unit test for this function because it does too many things
This 300-line function handles validation, business logic, persistence, and notification in a single block
Extracting smaller methods from this function would make its intent clear and each piece independently testable
A single method that has grown so long it is hard to understand, test, or reuse, and should be decomposed into smaller, well-named methods
explanation: A code smell where a method has grown too long, handling multiple responsibilities in a single block, making it hard to understand, test, and maintain.

clues: This file takes a long time to load in the IDE and is intimidating to new team members
The class has dozens of instance variables covering unrelated domains
It handles user management, reporting, notifications, and billing in one 3,000-line file
Multiple groups of methods in this class never use each other's instance variables, suggesting it should be split
The class could be decomposed into at least four smaller classes, each with a cohesive set of responsibilities
A class that has grown to contain too many instance variables and methods covering unrelated responsibilities, indicating it should be split into multiple focused classes
explanation: A code smell where a class has accumulated too many responsibilities, indicated by excessive instance variables and methods, suggesting it should be split into smaller, cohesive classes.

clues: A bug was fixed in one place but reappeared because the same logic exists elsewhere
Three different services have nearly identical validation logic, each with slightly different bugs
When the business rule changed, we had to update the same logic in five different files
Copy-pasted functions across modules have diverged over time, creating subtle behavioral inconsistencies
The same 40-line block of discount calculation logic appears verbatim in OrderService, CartService, and InvoiceService
Identical or near-identical code structures repeated in multiple locations instead of being extracted into a shared abstraction
explanation: A code smell where the same or very similar code exists in multiple places, making changes error-prone and leading to inconsistencies when one copy is updated but others are not.

clues: The codebase is larger than expected for what the application actually does
New developers waste time trying to understand functions that are never actually invoked
Code coverage reports show entire classes with zero execution, even in production
The feature was removed two years ago but the supporting code, routes, and database tables were never cleaned up
Static analysis reveals hundreds of functions that have no callers and no test coverage
Code that is never executed in any code path but remains in the codebase, adding confusion and maintenance burden without providing any value
explanation: Code that is no longer executed or reachable but remains in the codebase, increasing complexity and confusing developers without providing any value.

clues: Validation logic for the same kind of data is scattered throughout the codebase
Phone numbers, email addresses, and currency amounts are all passed around as plain strings
A bug occurred because a function received a price in cents where it expected dollars, and both were just integers
The zipCode parameter is a raw string, so invalid values like 'hello' pass through without validation until they hit the database
Instead of defining a Money class, the code passes around raw integers for amounts and strings for currency codes
Using primitive types like strings and integers to represent domain concepts instead of creating small, meaningful value objects that encapsulate validation and behavior
explanation: A code smell where primitive types are used to represent domain concepts instead of creating small value objects, leading to scattered validation and type-safety issues.

clues: Several method signatures in the codebase look suspiciously similar with the same group of parameters
The same three parameters always appear together across multiple function calls
Every function dealing with addresses takes street, city, state, and zip as four separate parameters
Changing the representation of a coordinate requires updating latitude and longitude parameters in 30+ function signatures
The parameters (startDate, endDate, timezone) always travel together but have never been grouped into a DateRange object
Groups of data items that repeatedly appear together in method signatures and field declarations, suggesting they should be extracted into their own object
explanation: A code smell where the same group of data items frequently appears together in method parameters and class fields, suggesting they should be encapsulated in their own class.

clues: Every time a new type is added to the system, several places in the code need to be updated
There are multiple switch statements scattered across the codebase that all branch on the same type field
Adding a new payment method requires updating switch statements in five different classes
The same type-based branching logic is duplicated across the service: one switch for processing, one for validation, one for display
Each switch block checks the same enum and performs a type-specific operation, a pattern that could be replaced with polymorphism
Repeated switch or if-else chains that dispatch on the same type discriminator, indicating that polymorphism should replace conditional branching
explanation: A code smell where similar switch statements on the same type appear in multiple places, suggesting the conditional logic should be replaced with polymorphism.

clues: The code is tightly coupled to the internal structure of objects it doesn't directly own
A change to an intermediate object breaks callers that are three levels of navigation away
The line of code reads like a sentence: get the manager of the department of the division of the company
The expression user.getAccount().getSettings().getPreferences().getTheme() chains four navigations deep
Any structural change in the middle of the chain breaks all callers, coupling the client to the entire object graph
A chain of getA().getB().getC() calls where the client navigates through multiple objects to reach the data it needs, violating the Law of Demeter
explanation: A code smell where a client asks one object for another, then asks that for another, creating a long chain of navigations that couples the client to the structure of the entire object graph.

clues: This class doesn't seem to do anything except pass calls through to another class
The wrapper adds no logic, no transformation, and no value — it just delegates every method
Half the methods in this class are one-liners that simply call the same method on the wrapped object
The ServiceFacade class has 20 methods, and every single one just calls the corresponding method on ServiceImpl
Over-delegation has created a class that exists only to forward calls, adding an unnecessary layer of indirection
A class where the majority of methods simply delegate to another class without adding any behavior, indicating an unnecessary layer of abstraction
explanation: A code smell where a class exists primarily to delegate calls to another class without adding meaningful behavior, creating unnecessary indirection.

clues: Changing the private internals of one class breaks another class that shouldn't know about them
Two classes are so tightly coupled that they can't be modified or tested independently
Class A directly accesses the private fields of Class B through reflection or friend access
The Order class reaches into the internal list of the Customer class to modify it directly instead of using a public API
These two classes are excessively coupled, with each one accessing the other's internal details, making independent evolution impossible
Two classes that are overly involved with each other's private details, accessing internal fields and methods that should be hidden, creating a fragile bidirectional coupling
explanation: A code smell where two classes are excessively coupled by accessing each other's private internals, making them impossible to change independently.

clues: The subclass overrides most of the parent's methods to do nothing or throw exceptions
It feels wrong that this class extends that class — they don't really have an is-a relationship
The Stack class extends ArrayList but methods like get(index) and set(index) make no sense for a stack
The subclass inherits 15 methods from its parent but only uses 3, leaving the rest as confusing, useless API surface
The child class throws UnsupportedOperationException for half the inherited methods because they violate its semantics
A subclass that inherits methods and data from its parent but doesn't actually want or use most of them, indicating the inheritance hierarchy is wrong
explanation: A code smell where a subclass inherits behavior from its parent but doesn't use or want most of it, suggesting the inheritance relationship is inappropriate.

clues: The codebase has elaborate abstractions that only have one implementation
The framework was designed to handle 'future requirements' that never materialized
There are three layers of interfaces and abstract classes for a feature that only has one concrete use case
The PluginArchitecture was built two years ago to allow extensibility, but only one plugin was ever written
Abstract factories, strategy patterns, and hook methods were introduced for flexibility that was never needed
Overly abstract, parameterized code built for anticipated future use cases that never materialized, adding complexity without providing current value
explanation: A code smell where abstractions and generality are introduced based on speculated future requirements that never materialize, adding unnecessary complexity.

clues: Some fields on this object are null most of the time and only populated in certain code paths
You can't trust the object's fields because some of them are only valid after certain methods are called
The class has fields that are only set during a specific calculation and are meaningless otherwise
The algorithm stores intermediate computation results in instance fields instead of local variables or a separate context object
The discountRate and taxAmount fields are only populated during the checkout flow and are null in every other context
Instance fields that are only set and used during a specific operation, remaining null or meaningless for the rest of the object's lifetime
explanation: A code smell where an object has fields that are only set under certain circumstances, being null or undefined the rest of the time, making the class harder to understand.

clues: Every time we add a new subclass in one hierarchy, we have to add a matching one in another
There are two parallel class trees that always change together, one for the model and one for the view
Adding a new vehicle type means creating VehicleType, VehicleTypeValidator, VehicleTypeSerializer, and VehicleTypeRenderer
The Shape hierarchy and the ShapeDrawer hierarchy mirror each other exactly, with a one-to-one correspondence
Every class in the domain hierarchy has a corresponding partner in the persistence hierarchy, and new types always require adding both
Two class hierarchies that mirror each other, where every new subclass in one hierarchy requires a corresponding new subclass in the other
explanation: A code smell where two inheritance hierarchies mirror each other, forcing every new class in one hierarchy to require a corresponding class in the other.

clues: This class doesn't seem to justify its own existence in the codebase
After a refactoring, the class was left with only one trivial method and no real responsibility
The StringHelper class contains a single method that just calls String.trim()
The class was created as part of an anticipated feature that was descoped, leaving it with almost no behavior
It has one field and one getter; its entire purpose could be served by the class that uses it
A class that does too little to justify its existence, typically having been shrunk by refactoring or created speculatively, and should be inlined into its callers
explanation: A code smell where a class does so little that it doesn't justify the cost of understanding and maintaining it, suggesting it should be inlined or merged with another class.

clues: This class is just a bag of fields with getters and setters, no real behavior
All the logic that operates on this class's data lives in other classes
The UserDTO has 12 fields and 24 accessor methods but zero business methods
Other classes reach into this object, pull out its fields, and perform calculations that should live on the object itself
The Order class has getters and setters for price, tax, and discount, but the actual calculation logic lives in OrderService
A class that contains only fields and accessors with no meaningful behavior, while the logic that should belong to it is placed in other classes
explanation: A code smell where a class has fields and accessors but no behavior, with all logic that manipulates its data living in external classes instead of on the object itself.

clues: The code is hard to read on its own, but there are extensive comments explaining what it does
Removing the comments would make the function completely incomprehensible
A long comment block explains a complex conditional, when the conditional itself should be extracted into a well-named method
The comment says 'check if user is eligible for discount' above a 20-line boolean expression that could be a method called isEligibleForDiscount()
Comments are being used to explain confusing code instead of rewriting the code to be self-explanatory
Comments used to explain poorly written code rather than improving the code itself, where renaming variables and extracting methods would eliminate the need for the comments
explanation: A code smell where comments are used to explain confusing or poorly structured code, when the real fix is to refactor the code to be self-explanatory through better naming and structure.

clues: This one class keeps changing for completely unrelated reasons
It was modified last week for a database migration, this week for a UI change, and next week for a new business rule
The same file is touched by the database team, the frontend team, and the billing team for different reasons
The Customer class changes when we add a new payment method, when we change the email format, and when we update tax rules
This class has multiple independent axes of change, suggesting it should be split along those axes
A class that is modified for many different, unrelated reasons because it contains multiple unrelated responsibilities that should be separated into distinct classes
explanation: A code smell where a single class is changed for many different reasons, indicating it has multiple responsibilities that should be separated into different classes.

clues: Calling this function requires remembering the order of many arguments, and it's easy to mix them up
The function signature wraps across three lines in the IDE
A new requirement added yet another parameter, bringing the total to 12
Callers frequently pass null for several parameters because those parameters only apply in certain cases
createUser(name, email, phone, street, city, state, zip, country, role, department, startDate, managerId) is called in 40 places
A method with so many parameters that it is difficult to call correctly, understand, and maintain, suggesting parameters should be grouped into objects
explanation: A code smell where a method takes too many parameters, making it hard to call correctly and understand, suggesting related parameters should be grouped into parameter objects.

clues: The app works on one developer's machine but fails on another because of a missing system library
There's no explicit declaration of all the libraries the application needs
The deployment relies on certain packages being pre-installed on the host operating system
The build succeeds locally because ImageMagick is installed globally, but fails in the CI container where it's not
The app implicitly depends on system-wide packages instead of explicitly declaring and isolating all dependencies in a manifest
The application relies on implicit system-level dependencies instead of explicitly declaring all dependencies in a manifest and isolating them from the system environment
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that apps should explicitly declare all dependencies and not rely on the implicit existence of system-wide packages.

clues: The application can't run without being deployed into a specific application server or container
We can't easily run two instances of the app because it's tightly coupled to its hosting environment
The app requires Tomcat to be pre-installed and configured instead of embedding its own HTTP server
Deploying to a different environment means reconfiguring the external web server rather than just starting the app
The application doesn't export HTTP as a service by binding to a port; instead it depends on an external runtime container to receive requests
The app fails to be self-contained by not embedding its own web server and binding to a port, instead requiring injection into an external application server
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that web apps should be self-contained, exporting HTTP by binding to a port, rather than requiring an external application server.

clues: Scaling to multiple instances breaks user sessions because each server stores its own state
After a deploy, users are logged out because the session data was stored in the process memory that was replaced
The app stores file uploads in the local filesystem and the files disappear when the container is replaced
Sticky sessions are required on the load balancer because the app caches user data in-process
The application stores session state, cached data, and uploaded files in local memory or disk instead of external backing services
Application processes store persistent state locally in memory or on the filesystem instead of using external stateless backing services, breaking horizontal scalability
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that processes should be stateless and share-nothing, with any persistent data stored in external backing services.

clues: Someone SSH'd into production and edited a config file directly to fix a bug
There's no clear separation between compiling the code and deploying it to an environment
Hotfixes are applied directly to running production servers instead of going through the build pipeline
The same script that compiles the code also deploys it to production with no distinct release stage
The build, release, and run stages are not strictly separated: code is modified in production and builds are not reproducible or versioned
The strict separation between building code, combining it with config into a release, and running that release is violated, making deployments irreproducible and auditing impossible
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that the build, release, and run stages should be strictly separated, with releases being immutable and deployable.

clues: It works on my machine but breaks in production
Bugs keep appearing in production that can never be reproduced locally
Development uses SQLite but production uses PostgreSQL, and queries behave differently between them
The local dev environment uses mock services while production uses real third-party APIs with different behavior
Development, staging, and production environments differ significantly in backing services, OS, and configuration, causing environment-specific bugs
Large gaps between development and production environments in backing services, operating systems, and data cause bugs that only manifest in production and cannot be reproduced locally
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that development, staging, and production should be as similar as possible to reduce the risk of environment-specific bugs.

clues: Logs are written to files on the local disk and are lost when the container is recycled
There's no centralized way to search or correlate logs across all service instances
The application manages its own log rotation and file writing instead of writing to stdout
Debugging requires SSH'ing into each of the 12 production containers to grep local log files
The app writes logs to /var/log/app.log with its own rotation logic instead of writing to stdout for the execution environment to capture
The application manages its own log files instead of treating logs as an event stream written to stdout, preventing the execution environment from handling routing, storage, and analysis
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that apps should write logs as an unbuffered event stream to stdout, letting the execution environment handle routing and storage.

clues: Deployments take 10 minutes because the app takes forever to start up and shut down
Killing the process results in data loss because it doesn't handle termination signals gracefully
In-flight requests are dropped during deploys because the app doesn't drain connections before shutting down
The application takes 3 minutes to initialize because it builds caches and loads data on startup instead of deferring
SIGTERM is not handled: the process is force-killed after timeout, leaving half-processed jobs in an inconsistent state
The application cannot be started or stopped quickly and gracefully, violating the principle that processes should be disposable with fast startup and graceful shutdown
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that processes should be disposable, starting fast and shutting down gracefully on SIGTERM, enabling rapid elastic scaling and deployment.

clues: We can only handle more traffic by adding RAM and CPU to a single machine
The application is designed to run as a single monolithic process and can't be horizontally scaled
Instead of running multiple small processes behind a load balancer, the app relies on a single multi-threaded process on a bigger server
Scaling is purely vertical because the app architecture uses a single process model instead of multiple process types
The app handles web requests, background jobs, and scheduled tasks in one process instead of using separate process types that can be independently scaled
The application scales vertically within a single process instead of horizontally across multiple processes of different types, violating the process model for concurrency
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that apps should scale out via the process model, using multiple process types for different workloads rather than scaling up a single process.

clues: Database migrations are run manually by SSH'ing into a production server
One-off admin tasks use a different codebase or environment than the main application
The data migration script was written against a local database schema that differs from production
Admin scripts run on a developer's laptop with a different ORM version than production, causing schema mismatches
One-off administrative processes like migrations and data fixes are run in ad-hoc environments instead of identical release environments
Administrative and management tasks are run in different environments and codebases than the application itself, instead of running as one-off processes in identical environments
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that admin processes like database migrations should run as one-off processes in the same environment and codebase as the application.

clues: Two services share code by copy-pasting from one repo to another, leading to drift
Different production servers run slightly different versions of the codebase
The same application has three separate Git repositories for three environments, and they've diverged
Some production customizations are maintained as patches on top of the main codebase in a separate repo
There is not a single codebase tracked in version control from which all deploys are derived
The application does not maintain a single codebase in version control with multiple deploys, instead using multiple repos or shared codebases that diverge
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that there should be exactly one codebase tracked in version control, with many deploys of that single codebase.

clues: Switching from one email provider to another requires extensive code changes
The database driver is deeply embedded in the business logic rather than abstracted behind an interface
Moving from a local SMTP server to a cloud email service requires modifying 20 files
The app uses vendor-specific database features directly in business logic, making it impossible to swap the database without a rewrite
The application treats local and third-party backing services differently instead of accessing all of them through uniform resource locators in configuration
The application is tightly coupled to specific backing service implementations instead of treating them as attached resources swappable via configuration changes
explanation: A violation of the twelve-factor principle that backing services should be treated as attached resources, accessible via a URL or config, and swappable without code changes.

clues: The disk I/O is much higher than expected given the volume of data being written by the application
SSD wear is significantly higher than the logical write volume, reducing the drive's lifespan
The storage engine performs compaction and merging that multiplies the amount of data actually written to disk
Inserting 1 GB of data causes 10 GB of physical writes because the LSM-tree has multiple compaction levels
Each logical write triggers multiple physical writes due to the storage engine's merge and compaction processes
The ratio of physical bytes written to disk versus logical bytes written by the application is much greater than one, caused by the storage engine's internal compaction and reorganization
explanation: The phenomenon where a single logical write to the database results in multiple physical writes to disk due to the storage engine's internal structures like LSM-trees or B-trees.

clues: One database shard is overloaded while the others are nearly idle
The partitioning scheme looked good in theory but traffic is concentrated on a single node
A celebrity user's activity causes all their followers' reads to hit the same partition
Partitioning by user_id puts a disproportionate amount of traffic on the partition containing the most active user
The partition key has a skewed distribution, causing one partition to receive orders of magnitude more traffic than the rest
A single partition in a distributed database receives a disproportionate share of reads or writes due to skewed key distribution, creating a bottleneck despite having multiple nodes
explanation: A problem in distributed databases where a single partition receives disproportionately more traffic than others due to skewed data or access patterns in the partition key.

clues: Two users edited the same record simultaneously and one user's changes disappeared without warning
The system silently discards one of two concurrent updates to the same key
There's no conflict detection; the most recent timestamp always overwrites earlier values
Timestamps from two different nodes resolved the conflict, but clock skew caused the older logical write to be kept
Concurrent writes to the same key are resolved by keeping only the value with the highest timestamp, silently discarding the other
A conflict resolution strategy where concurrent writes to the same key are resolved by comparing timestamps and keeping only the latest, silently dropping earlier writes regardless of causal order
explanation: A conflict resolution strategy in distributed systems where concurrent writes to the same key are resolved by keeping the write with the highest timestamp, potentially losing valid data.

clues: A read that occurs after a confirmed write sometimes returns the old value
Two clients reading the same key at the same time get different values, even after the write completed
The lock service appears to grant the same lock to two clients simultaneously
After a successful compare-and-swap, a subsequent read from another node returns the pre-swap value
The system does not provide the illusion of a single copy of the data with atomic operations, causing stale reads even after writes are acknowledged
The system fails to provide the guarantee that all operations appear to execute atomically in a single total order consistent with real time, allowing stale reads after confirmed writes
explanation: A consistency violation where the system fails to provide the guarantee that reads always reflect the most recent completed write, breaking the illusion of a single copy of the data.

clues: Users report that the UI shows conflicting information on different pages loaded at the same time
The shopping cart shows 3 items on one page and 2 items on another, moments apart
Developers assumed reads would immediately reflect writes, but the system only guarantees convergence over time
The frontend reads from a replica that hasn't received the latest write yet, showing the user a stale cart total
The system guarantees that all replicas will converge to the same value eventually, but provides no bound on when, causing confusion during the convergence window

Developers or users misunderstand the consistency model where replicas will eventually converge but may serve stale data in the interim, leading to surprising behavior during the replication delay window
explanation: The confusion that arises when developers or users expect strong consistency but the system provides eventual consistency, where replicas converge over time but may serve stale data.

clues: Two transactions each pass their validation checks individually but together produce an invalid state
The constraint is that at least one doctor must be on call, but two transactions simultaneously remove the last two
Each transaction reads a condition, decides to act, and writes, but the premise it checked is invalidated by the other transaction
The invariant is maintained by each transaction in isolation, but the combination of both transactions' writes violates it
Two concurrent transactions both read that the condition is safe, then both write changes that together violate the condition, because neither sees the other's uncommitted write

A concurrency anomaly where two transactions read the same data, make decisions based on what they read, and write, but the combination of their writes violates an invariant that neither individual write would break alone
explanation: A concurrency anomaly where two transactions each read overlapping data, make decisions based on it, and write, resulting in a state that violates an invariant even though each transaction individually was valid.

clues: A query returns different rows when re-executed within the same transaction
The transaction counted 5 matching rows, but when it re-runs the query, it finds 6
Another transaction inserted a new row that matches the first transaction's WHERE clause between its two reads
Row-level locks don't prevent this anomaly because the conflicting row didn't exist when the first query ran
A range query sees new rows that were inserted by another concurrent transaction after the first read but before the second

A concurrency anomaly where a transaction re-executes a range query and finds new rows that were inserted by another committed transaction, because the new rows didn't exist to be locked during the first read
explanation: A concurrency anomaly where a transaction re-executes a query and finds rows that were inserted by another transaction, because row-level locks cannot prevent inserts matching the query's predicate.

clues: Two users edited the same wiki page and one user's changes were silently overwritten
The application performs read-modify-write cycles without any concurrency control
Two concurrent increments of a counter result in only one increment being applied
Both transactions read the balance as $100, each adds $50, and both write $150, instead of the correct $200
The second write overwrites the first because neither transaction was aware of the other's concurrent modification
A concurrency anomaly where two transactions read the same value, modify it, and write it back, with the second write overwriting the first, losing the first transaction's update entirely
explanation: A concurrency anomaly where two transactions read the same value, independently modify it, and write back, with the later write overwriting the earlier one, losing its update.

clues: The distributed transaction is stuck and no participant can proceed or abort independently
All participants voted to commit, but the coordinator crashed before sending the final decision
Database resources are locked indefinitely because the transaction is in an uncertain state waiting for coordinator recovery
The participating nodes hold locks on rows and cannot release them until the coordinator comes back and tells them to commit or abort
After the coordinator crashes between the prepare and commit phases, participants are blocked holding locks with no way to resolve the transaction until the coordinator recovers

The distributed commit protocol blocks all participants indefinitely when the coordinator fails between the prepare and commit phases, because participants cannot safely commit or abort without the coordinator's decision
explanation: A failure mode of the two-phase commit protocol where participants become blocked holding locks when the coordinator crashes after participants have voted but before the commit/abort decision is delivered.

clues: The cluster has been unable to accept writes for several minutes while the nodes try to agree on a new primary
Network instability is causing the cluster to repeatedly start and fail the leader selection process
The old leader is still running but partitioned, while the remaining nodes cannot reach quorum to elect a new one
With two of five nodes unreachable, the remaining three cannot form a majority in some configurations, leaving the cluster leaderless
The consensus algorithm fails to elect a new leader due to network partitions, split votes, or loss of quorum, leaving the cluster unable to accept writes

The distributed consensus mechanism for selecting a single authoritative node fails due to network partitions, insufficient quorum, or repeated split votes, leaving the system without a leader and unable to process writes
explanation: A failure in distributed systems where the consensus algorithm for selecting a leader fails due to network issues, lack of quorum, or split votes, leaving the cluster unable to accept writes.

clues: A single user action triggers requests to an impractically large number of downstream services
The home timeline query is slow because it must aggregate data from hundreds of followed accounts in real time
A single API call triggers parallel requests to 50 microservices, and the overall latency is dominated by the slowest one
The social media feed construction reads from every followed user's post table at query time instead of pre-computing the timeline
A read-time fan-out to hundreds of partitions or services means latency is bounded by the slowest responder and tail latencies are amplified

A system design where a single logical operation requires contacting a large number of nodes or services, amplifying tail latency and making the operation's reliability the product of all downstream reliabilities
explanation: A scalability challenge where a single logical operation requires contacting many downstream services or partitions, amplifying tail latency and reducing overall reliability.

clues: After a production incident, team members seem reluctant to speak up about what happened
Engineers are crafting their incident reports to avoid mentioning specific names or decisions
The same class of failures keeps recurring because root causes aren't being fully explored
Management is asking 'who caused this?' instead of 'what allowed this to happen?'
Your incident review process focuses on punishment rather than systemic improvement
Teams need a structured review process that examines contributing factors without assigning individual fault
explanation: A blameless postmortem focuses on identifying systemic causes of incidents rather than blaming individuals, encouraging honest reporting and learning.

clues: Users are complaining but your dashboards all show green
Your team can't agree on what 'reliable enough' actually means for your service
The metrics you're tracking don't seem to correlate with user happiness
You're measuring server CPU utilization but users care about page load time
Your availability metric says 99.9% but customers are experiencing errors on critical workflows
You need to choose the right measurable proxy for user-perceived reliability of your service
explanation: SLI (Service Level Indicator) selection is the process of choosing metrics that accurately represent the quality of service experienced by users.

clues: Product and SRE teams keep clashing over whether to push new features or focus on reliability
There's no objective way to decide when to slow down feature releases
Your team burned through all reliability headroom last quarter but kept shipping anyway
Nobody knows what should happen when your service drops below its reliability target
You need a pre-agreed set of consequences for when cumulative errors exceed a threshold
Teams need a formal policy that defines actions to take when the allowed margin of unreliability is exhausted
explanation: An error budget policy defines what actions teams must take when they've consumed their allowed margin of unreliability, balancing feature velocity with reliability.

clues: A deployment went out and you didn't realize it was causing issues until all users were affected
Your team wants to catch regressions before they reach 100% of traffic
You're looking for a way to statistically compare a new version's behavior against the current one
A small percentage of traffic is routed to the new version but nobody is systematically evaluating the results
You need automated comparison of key metrics between a small new-version cohort and the baseline
Your deployment process lacks automated statistical analysis of a new release served to a small traffic slice
explanation: Canary analysis is the practice of automatically comparing metrics from a small percentage of traffic on a new release against the baseline to detect regressions.

clues: Every deployment is all-or-nothing and failures affect your entire user base immediately
You want to reduce blast radius when shipping changes
Your team discusses deploying to one region first but has no formal process for it
You need a structured way to incrementally increase the percentage of users on a new version
Your deployment should go 1% then 5% then 25% then 100% with health checks at each stage
You lack a phased deployment strategy that gradually increases exposure while validating at each step
explanation: Progressive rollout is a deployment strategy where changes are gradually released to increasing percentages of users, with validation gates between each phase.

clues: Your team needs to move users between service versions without downtime
Load balancer configuration changes are manual and error-prone during releases
You want fine-grained control over what percentage of requests go to which backend
Your blue-green deployment can't do anything between 0% and 100%
You need weighted routing that lets you gradually move traffic from v1 to v2 at the load balancer level
Your infrastructure lacks the ability to dynamically adjust request routing weights between service versions
explanation: Traffic shifting is the technique of gradually redirecting user requests from one service version to another using weighted routing at the load balancer or service mesh level.

clues: You're worried a new feature will break under real production load but can't reproduce it in staging
Your team needs to validate a backend change with real traffic without users seeing the results
Engineers want to shadow production requests to a new code path and compare outputs
The new recommendation engine needs to be tested with real queries but results must not be shown to users yet
You're forking live traffic to a new service and discarding its responses while measuring performance
You need a technique to exercise new code with real production traffic while keeping its output invisible to users
explanation: A dark launch is the practice of deploying new functionality and exercising it with real production traffic while keeping its results invisible to end users.

clues: Your codebase has become hard to reason about due to numerous conditional branches
Nobody remembers which experiments are still running and which were decided months ago
New engineers are confused by toggles that reference features launched a year ago
Your application has hundreds of flags and no process for cleaning up completed rollouts
Dead feature toggles litter the codebase, creating untested code paths and confusing logic
Accumulated stale feature flags that were never removed after their rollout completed are degrading code quality
explanation: Feature flag debt accumulates when teams add feature flags for rollouts but never remove them after the feature is fully launched, creating complexity and dead code paths.

clues: An issue that was fixed in staging reappears in production with no code changes
Servers that were set up identically months ago now behave differently
Someone SSH'd into a production box and made a manual change that isn't tracked anywhere
Your fleet of supposedly identical machines has subtle differences in installed packages and config files
The settings on your servers have gradually diverged from what's defined in your provisioning scripts
Manual ad-hoc changes to infrastructure over time have caused servers to deviate from their intended configuration state
explanation: Configuration drift occurs when the actual state of infrastructure gradually diverges from its intended state due to manual changes, patches, and ad-hoc fixes.

clues: Rebuilding your environment from scratch takes weeks of manual work
Only one person on the team knows how the production servers are actually configured
Changes to infrastructure aren't reviewed or version-controlled
You can't spin up a new environment that matches production because setup steps aren't documented
Critical infrastructure like load balancers and DNS were configured via web consoles with no code representation
Your infrastructure lacks machine-readable definitions that can be version-controlled, reviewed, and automatically applied
explanation: An infrastructure as code gap exists when critical infrastructure components are managed manually rather than through version-controlled, declarative configuration files.

clues: A routine library upgrade broke the entire build and nobody can figure out why
Two libraries your service needs require incompatible versions of the same transitive dependency
Your lock file has grown enormous and minor updates cascade into hundreds of changed packages
Upgrading package A requires upgrading package B which conflicts with package C
Your dependency tree has diamond conflicts where different libraries need mutually exclusive versions
Incompatible transitive dependency version requirements across your dependency graph make upgrades nearly impossible
explanation: Dependency hell is the situation where complex, conflicting, or circular dependency version requirements make it extremely difficult to install, upgrade, or maintain software.

clues: Requests between microservices are failing intermittently with no clear pattern
Retry storms are overwhelming downstream services after a sidecar proxy update
Mutual TLS between services broke silently after a certificate rotation
Traffic policies defined in YAML aren't being applied and requests bypass intended routing rules
Your Envoy sidecar proxies have conflicting timeout and circuit-breaker settings across services
The sidecar proxy layer managing inter-service communication has incorrect routing, retry, or mTLS configuration
explanation: Service mesh misconfiguration occurs when the sidecar proxy infrastructure managing service-to-service communication has incorrect settings for routing, retries, timeouts, or security policies.

clues: An incident took hours to diagnose because nobody could see what was happening inside the system
You have metrics dashboards but they don't answer the questions you need during an outage
Your monitoring tells you something is wrong but not why
You can see HTTP error rates but can't trace a single request through your microservice chain
Your system produces logs, metrics, and traces but they're not correlated and there are blind spots between services
Your system lacks the instrumentation needed to ask arbitrary questions about internal state and behavior during novel failures
explanation: An observability gap exists when a system's telemetry (logs, metrics, traces) is insufficient to diagnose novel or unexpected failures without deploying new instrumentation.

clues: Latency spikes occur in user-facing requests but you can't tell which backend service is slow
A request traverses twelve services and you lose visibility somewhere in the middle
Some services propagate trace context headers but others silently drop them
Your tracing system shows incomplete spans with missing parent-child relationships
Async message queue consumers don't attach incoming trace IDs, creating gaps in your distributed traces
Incomplete trace context propagation across your microservice architecture creates invisible segments in end-to-end request flows
explanation: A distributed tracing blindspot occurs when some services in a microservice architecture fail to propagate trace context, creating gaps in end-to-end request visibility.

clues: During an incident, engineers are SSH-ing into individual machines to read log files
You can't search logs across all instances of a service from a single interface
Important log entries from a crashed container were lost because they weren't shipped before the pod was terminated
Your centralized logging pipeline is silently dropping messages under high load
Fluentd buffers are overflowing and log entries from the critical incident window are missing from Elasticsearch
Your centralized log collection pipeline is failing to reliably ingest, store, or index logs from all services
explanation: Log aggregation failure occurs when the centralized log collection system drops, delays, or fails to index logs, forcing engineers to check individual machines during incidents.

clues: Your monitoring system is becoming slow and expensive but the number of services hasn't changed much
Prometheus is running out of memory and queries are timing out
Someone added a user_id label to a high-traffic metric and now there are millions of time series
Your TSDB storage costs doubled after a team added unbounded label values to their custom metrics
High-cardinality labels like request IDs or email addresses on metrics are creating millions of unique time series
Unbounded label dimensions on metrics have caused a combinatorial explosion in the number of stored time series
explanation: Metric cardinality explosion occurs when metrics have labels with unbounded or very high cardinality values, causing the number of unique time series to grow uncontrollably.

clues: On-call engineers keep waking up senior staff to ask how to handle alerts
Every incident response looks different depending on who happens to be on-call
A new team member got paged and had no idea what steps to take for a database failover alert
Your alerts fire but there are no documented procedures linked to them explaining what to do
Pages go out at 3 AM and the responder has to reverse-engineer the remediation from scratch each time
Your alerting system lacks associated step-by-step operational procedures for diagnosing and resolving known failure modes
explanation: Runbook absence is the lack of documented, step-by-step operational procedures linked to alerts, forcing on-call engineers to improvise responses to known failure scenarios.

clues: During major incidents, too many people are talking and nobody is coordinating the response
Critical information gets lost in a noisy Slack channel with fifty participants during an outage
Three different engineers are trying different fixes simultaneously without telling each other
Your incident response bridge call has no structure and people talk over each other
Multiple conflicting remediation attempts are happening in parallel with no central coordination during a P1 incident
Your major incident response lacks role assignment, structured communication, and coordinated decision-making
explanation: War room chaos occurs during major incidents when too many responders participate without clear roles, structured communication, or coordinated decision-making.

clues: A critical alert went unacknowledged for 45 minutes because the primary on-call was asleep
Nobody knew who to contact when the issue was beyond the on-call engineer's expertise
The paging system doesn't automatically notify a backup when the first responder doesn't answer
An incident required database expertise but the escalation path only included application engineers
Your alerting has no secondary or tertiary contacts and no automatic escalation after acknowledgment timeout
Your on-call system lacks defined escalation tiers, automatic backup notification, and expertise-based routing for unacknowledged alerts
explanation: Escalation policy failure occurs when the on-call system lacks proper escalation chains, automatic fallback to backup responders, and routing to appropriate expertise.

clues: During an outage, nobody is making final decisions and the team is going in circles
Engineers are each pursuing their own theory of the root cause with no coordination
Status updates to stakeholders are inconsistent because nobody owns external communication
Your incident response has workers but no leader delegating tasks and tracking progress
A major P1 outage has ten engineers responding but nobody is assigning roles or making authoritative decisions
Your incident response process lacks a designated leader responsible for coordinating response, making decisions, and communicating status
explanation: Incident commander absence is the lack of a designated leader during incident response who coordinates efforts, makes decisions, delegates tasks, and manages communication.

clues: Incidents that used to be resolved in minutes now take hours
Your system's ability to bounce back from failures has gotten worse over the past year
New architecture complexity means that diagnosing and fixing issues takes longer than it used to
Rollback procedures that were once quick now involve coordinating across five teams
Your p50 recovery time has increased from 15 minutes to 2 hours over the past six months
The average time between detecting a failure and restoring service has been steadily increasing across incident reports
explanation: Mean time to recovery (MTTR) degradation is the gradual increase in the average time required to restore service after a failure, indicating declining operational capability.

clues: One component going down took your entire application offline
Your architecture has bottlenecks where one failure cascades to everything
The system has no redundancy for a critical path component
When your single Redis instance crashed, every microservice that depended on it went down simultaneously
Your authentication service runs on one unreplicated instance and its failure locks out all users
A critical component in your architecture has no redundancy, and its failure alone causes a complete system outage
explanation: A single point of failure is a component with no redundancy whose failure alone causes the entire system or a critical function to become unavailable.

clues: You have redundancy set up but you've never actually tested whether it works
The database replica was supposed to take over automatically but when the primary died, nothing happened
Your disaster recovery plan exists on paper but has never been executed
The last time someone tested the backup restore process was two years ago and the procedure is outdated
Your multi-region failover was never exercised and when a real regional outage hit, DNS failover didn't work
Redundancy and failover mechanisms exist in your architecture but have never been validated through actual failure simulation
explanation: A failover testing gap exists when redundancy and disaster recovery mechanisms are in place but have never been tested with real failure scenarios to verify they work correctly.

clues: Your team talks about resilience but is afraid to deliberately test failure scenarios in production
Leadership vetoed a proposal to inject faults because 'we can't risk breaking things'
The team only discovers failure modes during real outages, never proactively
Engineers proposed killing random pods in production to test recovery but got pushback from management
Your organization refuses to adopt controlled fault injection experiments because of perceived risk to production stability
Cultural and organizational resistance prevents your team from running controlled failure experiments to proactively discover weaknesses
explanation: Chaos engineering resistance is organizational pushback against deliberately injecting failures into production systems to proactively discover resilience weaknesses.

clues: Your engineering team spends more time firefighting than building
On-call rotations are burning people out and nobody has time for project work
Toil from manual operational tasks has consumed most of the team's engineering capacity
The SRE team is spending 80% of their time on repetitive operational work instead of the recommended maximum of 50%
Pager volume, manual deployments, and ticket queues have consumed all available engineering time leaving nothing for automation or improvement
