A pod enters CrashLoopBackOff because it crashes on every start and Kubernetes exponentially increases the delay between restart attempts
explanation: CrashLoopBackOff is a Kubernetes state where a container repeatedly crashes on startup and the kubelet delays restarts with exponential backoff.

clues: The main application container never starts even though its image is correct
Pod status shows it is stuck in the Init phase indefinitely
A prerequisite step that runs before the application is failing silently
The pod has an initialization container that is supposed to run database migrations before the app starts
The init container cannot connect to the database because network policies block its egress
An init container that must complete before the main app starts is failing due to a misconfigured dependency, preventing the pod from ever reaching the Running state
explanation: Init container failure occurs when a prerequisite container that must complete before the main application starts encounters an error, blocking the entire pod from launching.

clues: Background job processing has slowed to a crawl
Workers are busy but the overall throughput has dropped dramatically
The same jobs keep appearing at the front of the queue over and over
A few malformed messages cause workers to crash and the messages get requeued
Workers pick up a corrupted job, fail, the job returns to the queue, and the cycle repeats indefinitely
Poison messages that always cause processing failures are being endlessly retried in the queue, starving all other jobs of worker capacity
explanation: Job queue poisoning occurs when messages that consistently cause processing failures are repeatedly retried, blocking the queue and consuming worker resources.

clues: Failed messages are being silently dropped and nobody noticed for weeks
The error handling path for async processing has no monitoring or alerts
A queue designated for unprocessable messages has been growing unattended
The dead letter queue has exceeded its storage limit and is now rejecting new entries
Millions of failed payment webhook events accumulated in the DLQ with no alerting, and new failures are being lost
The dead letter queue for failed messages overflowed because no one monitored its size or reprocessed its contents, causing permanent data loss
explanation: Dead letter queue overflow occurs when failed messages accumulate in a DLQ without monitoring or reprocessing, eventually exceeding capacity and losing messages.

clues: A nightly batch process is producing duplicate records in the database
Resource usage spikes are occurring because multiple instances of the same task run simultaneously
The job that should take 30 minutes is still running when the next scheduled execution starts
There is no mutex or locking mechanism to prevent concurrent execution of the same scheduled task
The CronJob concurrencyPolicy is set to Allow, so a new instance starts every hour even if the previous one is still running
A scheduled job that takes longer than its interval runs overlapping instances because no concurrency guard prevents duplicate executions
explanation: Cron job overlap occurs when a scheduled job takes longer than its interval and no concurrency control prevents the next invocation from starting before the previous one finishes.

clues: A large data processing job keeps failing without completing
The job processes most of the data successfully but gets killed before finishing
Increasing the data volume has made a previously reliable job unreliable
The job exceeds its allocated execution window as the dataset grows
The Kubernetes Job has a 1-hour activeDeadlineSeconds but the dataset now requires 90 minutes to process
A batch job that processes growing data volumes exceeds its timeout limit and gets killed before completion, leaving partial results
explanation: Batch processing timeout occurs when a job's execution time grows with data volume but its timeout remains fixed, eventually causing incomplete processing.

clues: Data freshness in the analytics warehouse is degrading week over week
One stage in the data pipeline takes significantly longer than all others
Scaling up parallel workers for the fast stages has not improved end-to-end throughput
The transform step is single-threaded and processes records sequentially
A complex JSON-to-relational transformation stage runs on a single node while extract and load stages are parallelized across 10
One stage in the extract-transform-load pipeline is orders of magnitude slower than the others, bottlenecking the entire pipeline regardless of how much the other stages are scaled
explanation: An ETL pipeline bottleneck occurs when one phase of the pipeline is disproportionately slow, limiting overall throughput regardless of capacity added to other phases.

clues: Upstream producers are running out of memory trying to buffer unprocessed data
A downstream consumer is slower than the rate at which data arrives
The message broker's disk usage is growing rapidly during peak hours
There is no mechanism for the slow consumer to signal the fast producer to slow down
Kafka consumer lag for the analytics topic is growing by millions of messages per hour during peak traffic
A fast data producer overwhelms a slow consumer because the pipeline lacks a backpressure mechanism to throttle the producer when the consumer falls behind
explanation: Data pipeline backpressure failure occurs when there is no flow control mechanism to slow producers when consumers cannot keep up, leading to unbounded buffering or data loss.

clues: The codebase has classes that seem to do very little useful work
Some objects are created only to immediately delegate to another object
Removing a particular class and calling its dependency directly would simplify the code
The class has one method that just passes its arguments to another class's method
A UserManager class exists whose only method is createUser() which calls UserService.create() with the same parameters
A short-lived class exists solely to invoke methods on another class, adding indirection without logic, state, or value
explanation: A poltergeist class is a short-lived object with no real responsibility that merely delegates to other classes, adding unnecessary indirection to the design.

clues: One class in the codebase has grown to over 3000 lines
New features keep getting added to the same class because it already has the data they need
The class has dozens of unrelated public methods
Developers joke that every feature request somehow ends up modifying this class
The ApplicationHelper class handles logging, caching, email sending, PDF generation, and user authentication
A single class has accumulated so many unrelated responsibilities that it has become a monolithic utility doing everything, making it impossible to modify one concern without risking others
explanation: A Swiss Army Knife class takes on too many responsibilities, becoming a god object that is difficult to understand, test, and change safely.

clues: Adding a new type of entity requires modifying a deeply nested inheritance hierarchy
Subclasses inherit methods they do not need or cannot meaningfully use
The class hierarchy is more than five levels deep
New requirements keep forcing changes to the base class that break existing subclasses
A Vehicle base class has Car, Truck, and Bicycle subclasses, but adding Boat requires adding waterNavigation() to Vehicle which makes no sense for Car
An excessive inheritance hierarchy forces subclasses to inherit irrelevant behavior from base classes, creating fragile coupling across the hierarchy
explanation: Baseclass overuse occurs when inheritance hierarchies grow too deep or broad, forcing unrelated concepts into a rigid tree structure that resists change.

clues: Methods appear on a class but nobody can find where they are defined by looking at the class itself
Name collisions between included modules cause subtle bugs
A class includes seven different modules and the method resolution order is unpredictable
Developers cannot reason about the class interface without tracing through all its mixed-in modules
A User model includes Authenticatable, Searchable, Cacheable, Auditable, Taggable, and Notifiable mixins that all add methods and callbacks to the same class
Too many mixins are composed into a single class, creating an implicit and unpredictable interface where method conflicts and ordering issues cause hard-to-trace bugs
explanation: Mixin abuse occurs when too many modules are composed into a class, creating an implicit interface that is hard to understand and prone to name collisions and ordering bugs.

clues: Changing a parent class unexpectedly breaks several unrelated child classes
The class hierarchy was designed around what objects are rather than what they do
A class inherits behavior it does not need just to reuse a few methods from the parent
Refactoring is painful because extracting a class from the hierarchy would break the chain

Replicas executing a non-commutative sequence of writes produce different results because the broadcast does not guarantee all nodes deliver messages in the same total order
The atomic broadcast layer fails to guarantee that all nodes deliver messages in the same total order, causing state machine replicas to diverge when operations are non-commutative
explanation: Atomic broadcast ordering issues arise when the messaging layer cannot guarantee that all nodes deliver messages in the exact same total order, breaking state machine replication.

clues: Write operations have unexpectedly high latency even though the individual nodes are fast
Every write must wait for a global ordering step before it can be applied
The latency of a single write is bounded by the time to reach agreement across all nodes
Geographically distributed nodes make the ordering step particularly slow
Each write requires a cross-datacenter round trip to the sequencer node in another continent, adding 200ms to every operation
Total order broadcast adds significant latency because every message must be globally sequenced before delivery, and this coordination cost scales with network distance between nodes
explanation: Total order broadcast delay is the inherent latency cost of globally ordering every message before delivery, which is especially painful in geographically distributed systems.

clues: The system can tell that event A happened before event B but cannot tell if two events are truly concurrent
Timestamps assign a total order but lose information about true concurrency
Two causally unrelated events are given an ordering that implies one happened before the other
The logical clock cannot distinguish between events that are causally related and those that merely have different counter values
Lamport timestamps assigned sequential numbers to two independent events on different nodes, making it impossible to tell they were actually concurrent
Lamport timestamps provide a total order consistent with causality but cannot identify truly concurrent events because they do not capture the partial order of causality
explanation: Lamport timestamps define a total order compatible with causal order but lose information about concurrency, since they cannot distinguish concurrent events from causally ordered ones.

clues: The logical clock component is advancing far ahead of the physical wall clock
Timestamps in the system no longer roughly correspond to real time
A node that received a message with a very high timestamp had its hybrid clock jump forward permanently
The logical counter keeps incrementing without the physical component catching up
A misconfigured node sent an HLC timestamp with a wall clock 1 year in the future, causing every node that received its messages to permanently advance their hybrid clock to that future time
A hybrid logical clock drifted far from real time because a message from a node with a skewed physical clock propagated its inflated timestamp, permanently advancing the HLC on all receiving nodes
explanation: Hybrid logical clock drift occurs when a node with a skewed physical clock propagates inflated timestamps to other nodes, permanently pushing their HLC values away from real time.

clues: Two transactions that should see consistent state are making decisions based on stale reads
A write skew anomaly occurred even though each transaction read a consistent snapshot
Two doctors both read that the other is on call and both decide to go off call, leaving no one on duty
The isolation level prevents dirty reads and non-repeatable reads but allows this type of anomaly
Under snapshot isolation, two concurrent transactions each read the same snapshot, both decide to modify different rows based on the same premise, and the combined result violates an invariant
Snapshot isolation allows write skew anomalies where two transactions read overlapping data, make disjoint writes based on the same stale snapshot, and together violate a business constraint
explanation: Snapshot isolation anomalies like write skew occur when concurrent transactions make decisions based on the same snapshot and their combined writes violate an invariant.

clues: The database provides full serializability but the abort rate is unexpectedly high
Many transactions are being rolled back and retried, reducing effective throughput
Read-heavy transactions with long durations are disproportionately likely to be aborted
The system detects potential serialization anomalies optimistically and aborts transactions that might violate serializability
SSI aborts 30% of transactions during peak load because read-write conflicts detected at commit time force rollbacks, even though most aborted transactions would not have actually caused anomalies
Serializable snapshot isolation detects potential conflicts optimistically and aborts transactions at commit time, causing high abort rates under contention that negate its concurrency benefits
explanation: SSI overhead manifests as high transaction abort rates because the optimistic conflict detection can produce false positives, aborting transactions that may not have caused actual anomalies.

clues: Two transactions are stuck waiting for each other to release locks
Query throughput has dropped to zero for certain tables
The database's deadlock detector killed a transaction after a timeout
Transaction A holds a lock on row X and waits for row Y, while Transaction B holds a lock on row Y and waits for row X
Under 2PL, Transaction A acquired a shared lock on account 1 and wants to upgrade to exclusive, while Transaction B did the same on account 2 and each waits for the other's lock
Two-phase locking caused a deadlock where two transactions each hold locks that the other needs, forming a cycle that prevents both from making progress
explanation: Two-phase locking deadlocks occur when transactions form a circular dependency of lock requests, requiring one to be aborted to break the cycle.

clues: The system retries transactions very frequently under high contention
During peak hours, more transactions are aborted and retried than successfully committed
The approach works well at low contention but degrades severely as write conflicts increase
No locks are acquired but the commit validation frequently fails
Under optimistic concurrency control, the hot product inventory row causes 70% of checkout transactions to abort at validation because they all read and write the same row

Optimistic concurrency control suffers from excessive abort rates when many transactions contend for the same data, because conflicts are only detected at commit time and all conflicting transactions must retry
explanation: Optimistic concurrency control abort rate problems arise when high contention causes most transactions to fail validation at commit time, wasting the work they performed.

clues: Database storage usage is growing much faster than the actual data volume
Tables are consuming far more disk space than their logical data size would suggest
Old row versions are accumulating because long-running transactions prevent cleanup
The autovacuum process cannot reclaim dead tuples fast enough
A long-running analytics query holds a snapshot open for hours, preventing PostgreSQL from vacuuming 50GB of dead row versions from a heavily updated table
MVCC retains multiple versions of each row for concurrent readers, and long-running transactions prevent old versions from being garbage collected, causing unbounded storage growth
explanation: MVCC bloat occurs when old row versions cannot be garbage collected because long-running transactions still reference them, causing excessive storage consumption.

clues: The database server's disk is filling up with log files
Storage consumption is dominated by transaction logs rather than actual data
Replication lag is preventing old log segments from being archived and deleted
The log files cannot be truncated because a lagging replica still needs them
PostgreSQL WAL segments are accumulating at 1GB per hour because a replica 12 hours behind prevents wal_keep_size cleanup, consuming 15GB of disk
Write-ahead log files are growing unboundedly because log retention policies or lagging consumers prevent old segments from being reclaimed
explanation: WAL growth occurs when write-ahead log segments cannot be recycled because of lagging replicas, backup processes, or misconfigured retention, consuming excessive disk space.

clues: The database experiences periodic latency spikes at regular intervals
I/O utilization maxes out every few minutes for a brief period
The spikes correlate with the database flushing dirty pages to disk
All modified pages in the buffer pool are being written at once instead of gradually
PostgreSQL checkpoint_completion_target is set to 0.1, causing checkpoints to flush all dirty pages in a burst that saturates disk I/O and stalls queries
The database performs checkpoints too aggressively, flushing a large number of dirty pages to disk in a short burst that causes I/O saturation and periodic latency spikes
explanation: A checkpoint storm occurs when the database flushes dirty pages to disk too aggressively during checkpoints, causing I/O saturation and periodic performance degradation.

clues: Database recovery after a crash is taking much longer than expected
The system is replaying a large volume of logged transactions before it can accept new connections
Time to recover is proportional to the amount of work done since the last checkpoint
Increasing the checkpoint interval improved write performance but made recovery slower
After a crash, PostgreSQL spends 45 minutes replaying WAL records accumulated since the last checkpoint 2 hours ago before accepting connections
Redo log replay during crash recovery takes excessively long because the interval between checkpoints is too large, requiring replay of a massive volume of WAL records
explanation: Redo log replay delay occurs during crash recovery when the database must replay a large volume of WAL records since the last checkpoint, extending downtime.

clues: The team needs to recover data to a specific moment but discovers they cannot
Base backups exist but the continuous log archive has a gap
WAL segments needed for recovery were deleted before being archived
The backup strategy allows full restores but not recovery to an arbitrary timestamp
A 2-hour gap in WAL archiving means the team cannot restore to any point during the incident window, only to the last full backup 24 hours ago
A gap in the continuous write-ahead log archive makes point-in-time recovery impossible for a critical window because the chain of WAL segments is broken
explanation: A point-in-time recovery gap occurs when missing WAL segments break the continuous archive chain, preventing recovery to any timestamp within the gap.

clues: A backup restoration failed because one link in a chain of dependent backups was corrupted
The restore process depends on successfully applying every backup in sequence
A single corrupted incremental backup invalidates all subsequent incrementals
The backup strategy saves space but creates a fragile dependency chain
The full backup is from 30 days ago and 29 daily incrementals must all be applied in order, but day 15's increment is corrupted, making days 15-29 unrestorable
A long chain of incremental backups creates a fragile dependency where corruption of any single increment in the chain makes all subsequent increments useless for restore
explanation: Incremental backup chains are fragile because each backup depends on all previous ones, so a single corrupted link invalidates the entire chain from that point forward.

clues: Adding more read replicas is not improving read throughput as expected
The primary node's network bandwidth is saturated from sending replication streams
Replication lag is increasing as more replicas are added to the cluster
All replicas are connected directly to the primary, creating a fan-out bottleneck
The primary sends a separate replication stream to each of 20 replicas, consuming 80% of its network bandwidth and causing all replicas to lag
A star replication topology where all replicas connect directly to the primary creates a fan-out bottleneck that saturates the primary's network as replica count grows
explanation: Replication topology bottleneck occurs when all replicas connect directly to the primary, creating a fan-out that saturates the primary's resources as the number of replicas grows.

clues: Users in different regions are seeing their edits overwritten by other users' changes
Data modified in one datacenter is conflicting with modifications in another
The same record was updated in two locations simultaneously and only one version survived
The conflict resolution strategy is last-write-wins, silently dropping one update
A user updates their profile in US-East and simultaneously in EU-West, and the last-write-wins conflict resolution silently discards one update with no merge
Multi-leader replication allows concurrent writes to the same record at different leaders, and the conflict resolution strategy silently drops one write instead of merging them
explanation: Multi-leader write conflicts occur when the same data is modified at different leaders concurrently and the conflict resolution strategy loses data, often via last-write-wins.

clues: Reads are returning stale data even though the system is configured for quorum reads
The quorum parameters do not actually guarantee overlap between read and write sets
The cluster has 5 nodes but the read and write quorum values were set incorrectly
w + r is not greater than n, so there is no guarantee a read contacts a node that has the latest write
With n=5, w=2, r=2, there is no guarantee of overlap between the write set and read set since w+r=4 which is less than n+1=6
The leaderless quorum parameters w and r were configured such that w + r <= n, which means reads are not guaranteed to see the latest write because read and write quorums may not overlap
explanation: Leaderless quorum miscalculation occurs when w + r <= n, meaning the read quorum and write quorum do not necessarily overlap, allowing reads to miss recent writes.

clues: Quorum reads sometimes return stale data despite correct quorum parameters
The system satisfies w + r > n but still serves inconsistent reads
Concurrent writes during a read can cause the quorum to return a mix of old and new values
Sloppy quorums allow reads to be satisfied by nodes that may not have the latest data
With sloppy quorum enabled, a read quorum of 3 was satisfied by 2 regular nodes and 1 hinted-handoff node that has not yet received the latest write
Despite correct quorum math, sloppy quorums or concurrent write timing can cause reads to return stale data because the responding nodes may not include one that has the latest version
explanation: Read quorum inconsistency occurs when sloppy quorums or concurrent writes allow a quorum read to be satisfied by nodes that do not hold the latest value.

clues: A node that was down for a while is very slow to catch up when it rejoins
Other nodes in the cluster are using more disk space than expected
Writes meant for an unavailable node are being stored temporarily on other nodes
The temporary storage for hints is growing because the target node has been down for days
Node C has been offline for 72 hours and nodes A and B have accumulated 50GB of hinted handoff data waiting to be delivered, consuming their local disk
Hinted handoff queues grow unboundedly on healthy nodes when a replica is offline for an extended period, consuming disk space and creating a large backlog to replay on recovery
explanation: Hinted handoff queue growth occurs when writes destined for an offline replica accumulate on other nodes, consuming their disk and creating a large replay backlog.

clues: Background synchronization between replicas is consuming significant CPU and I/O
The hash tree comparison process runs continuously and impacts query performance
Every key range has its hash recomputed regularly to detect inconsistencies between replicas
Rebuilding the tree structure after writes is expensive because every write changes hashes up to the root
Cassandra's Merkle tree repair process reads every row to recompute hashes, consuming 40% of I/O budget on a 2TB dataset and slowing foreground reads
Anti-entropy repair using Merkle trees consumes excessive I/O and CPU because the tree must be rebuilt by reading all data to recompute hashes, impacting foreground workload performance
explanation: Anti-entropy Merkle tree overhead arises because building and comparing hash trees requires reading all data in the replicated range, consuming significant I/O and CPU resources.

clues: Engineers feel like they spend most of their time on repetitive work instead of projects
Sprint velocity keeps dropping even though the team size hasn't changed
Managers notice that automation projects never get started because the team is always busy
A tracking exercise reveals that over 60% of engineering time goes to operational tasks
The SRE team has exceeded the agreed-upon threshold where manual, repetitive work should trigger automation investment
The team has blown past the 50% ceiling on toil defined in their operating agreement and no one raised the alarm
explanation: Toil budget violation occurs when the proportion of manual, repetitive operational work exceeds the agreed-upon limit, starving engineering time for automation and improvement.

clues: Releases to production only happen on certain days of the week
A specific senior engineer must be present for every release
The team follows a lengthy checklist with dozens of manual steps for each deploy
Deployments take hours and involve multiple teams coordinating over a video call
A 37-step wiki page must be followed precisely to push code live, and skipping a step once caused an outage
Every production release is a high-stress ritual requiring manual approvals, hand-edited configs, and a designated 'release captain'
explanation: Manual deployment ceremony is when releases become elaborate manual rituals instead of automated, repeatable processes, increasing risk and reducing deployment frequency.

clues: Tasks seem to stall at certain points in the workflow for no obvious reason
One team's queue is always full while other teams wait for them
Cross-team tickets have a much longer average resolution time than intra-team tickets
A single operations group must approve every change before it reaches the next stage
Requests pile up at the boundary between development and operations because only one person reviews them
Work items systematically stall at inter-team transition points because the receiving team lacks context and capacity to process incoming requests
explanation: Handoff bottlenecks occur when work systematically stalls at transition points between teams or roles, creating queues and delays due to context loss and capacity mismatches.

clues: Some parts of the system seem to work by magic because nobody can explain them
New hires take months to become productive on certain components
When a particular engineer goes on vacation, certain types of incidents take much longer to resolve
Only one team understands the billing pipeline and they refuse to share documentation
Critical system knowledge is locked within a single team's boundary and no cross-training has ever been attempted
An entire subsystem's operational knowledge exists only within one group, and their departure would leave the organization unable to maintain it
explanation: Knowledge silos form when critical system understanding becomes trapped within a single team or group, creating organizational risk and slowing cross-team collaboration.

clues: New team members often get stuck on tasks that veterans find trivial
There's a lot of 'you just have to know' when it comes to operating the system
An engineer's departure causes a sudden spike in incident resolution times
Critical procedures exist only in the heads of long-tenured staff members
The team discovered during a postmortem that the fix required knowledge only one person had, and that person learned it from someone who already left
Essential operational procedures and system quirks are transmitted orally between engineers rather than being documented, creating single points of failure when people leave
explanation: Tribal knowledge dependency occurs when critical operational information is passed informally between individuals rather than being formally documented, creating fragile human single points of failure.

clues: Engineers complain that internal docs are unhelpful
New hires follow the getting-started guide and end up with a broken setup
The architecture diagrams reference services that were decommissioned two years ago
Following the official runbook during an incident made things worse because the steps were outdated
The wiki page for the authentication service still describes the OAuth 1.0 flow that was replaced eighteen months ago
Documentation has drifted so far from reality that engineers actively warn newcomers not to trust the internal wiki
explanation: Documentation rot is the gradual divergence between written documentation and actual system behavior, making docs unreliable or even dangerous to follow.

clues: On-call engineers seem to handle the same alerts differently each time
Incident responders frequently skip the documented procedures
A new team member followed the prescribed steps during an outage and accidentally made it worse
The remediation steps reference a CLI tool that was deprecated and removed six months ago
The runbook for the database failover procedure still references the old primary host that was replaced during last quarter's migration
Operational runbooks contain procedures for infrastructure that no longer exists, with commands targeting decommissioned hosts and deprecated tooling
explanation: Runbook staleness occurs when operational runbooks fall out of date with the actual system, turning them from helpful guides into potentially dangerous misinformation during incidents.

clues: The incident response process hasn't changed in years despite major system evolution
Teams follow rigid procedures even when they clearly don't apply to the current situation
Engineers complain that the standard operating procedures slow them down during outages
During a novel failure mode, responders wasted time trying to match it to an existing procedure instead of thinking critically
The team's response playbooks haven't been updated since the monolith was decomposed into microservices, yet they still prescribe monolith-era remediation steps
Incident response procedures have calcified into unchangeable rituals that no one dares modify, even though the system architecture they were written for no longer exists
explanation: Playbook ossification is when incident response procedures become rigid and outdated, unable to evolve with the system, causing responders to follow inappropriate steps during novel incidents.

clues: The team seems surprised by outages that follow a predictable pattern
Postmortems keep identifying the same root causes quarter after quarter
Nobody noticed that the last three major incidents all involved the same subsystem
The team treats each capacity-related outage as a unique event rather than a recurring theme
Review of the past year's incidents reveals that 40% share the same underlying failure mode, but each postmortem treated it as novel
The organization fails to recognize recurring incident patterns across postmortems, treating systemic issues as isolated events and never addressing the common root cause
explanation: Incident pattern blindness is the failure to recognize recurring themes across incidents, causing teams to repeatedly address symptoms rather than systemic root causes.

clues: The team often says 'we got lucky' after close calls but doesn't follow up
Minor issues that almost caused outages are closed without action items
An engineer noticed a race condition in production but since nothing broke, it wasn't prioritized
A config change nearly took down the payment system but was reverted quickly, so no postmortem was held
The team's auto-scaler hit its ceiling during a traffic spike and barely held on, but since no customers noticed, it was marked as a non-issue
Events where the system narrowly avoided failure are systematically dismissed as non-incidents, missing critical opportunities to strengthen resilience before actual outages occur
explanation: Near-miss dismissal is the pattern of ignoring or downplaying events where failure was narrowly avoided, missing valuable opportunities to learn and improve before a real outage occurs.

clues: The on-call team feels overwhelmed even though the number of incidents hasn't increased
Almost every alert triggers the highest-urgency response process
Engineers have started ignoring severity-1 pages because most of them turn out to be minor issues
A real critical outage was initially slow to get attention because responders assumed it was another false alarm
90% of tickets filed in the last month were marked as severity-1, making it impossible to distinguish genuine emergencies from routine issues
Teams routinely classify minor issues at the highest severity level to get faster response, eroding the meaning of severity classifications and causing alert fatigue for genuine emergencies
explanation: Severity inflation occurs when teams consistently overclassify incident severity to get faster attention, degrading the usefulness of severity levels and causing fatigue for real emergencies.

clues: The team seems to work on the wrong things during outages
A minor cosmetic bug got more engineering attention than a degraded core service
During a multi-issue incident, the team focused on the easiest problem rather than the most impactful one
Engineers spent hours debugging a non-critical logging issue while the checkout flow was failing for 10% of users
An incident commander allocated the senior database engineer to investigate a minor UI glitch while a junior engineer struggled alone with a cascading database failure
During incidents, lower-impact issues receive more attention and resources than higher-impact ones, inverting the natural priority order and prolonging customer-facing outages
explanation: Priority inversion in incidents occurs when less critical issues receive more attention than more critical ones during an active incident, prolonging the impact of the most severe problems.

clues: People involved in incident response seem to be working at cross purposes
Two engineers independently made conflicting changes to the same system during an outage
The incident took twice as long to resolve because teams were duplicating each other's investigation work
The network team rolled back a config change at the same time the application team was deploying a fix that depended on it
During the postmortem, responders discovered that three separate engineers had identified the root cause independently but none communicated it to the incident channel
Incident responders operate in isolation without sharing findings, leading to duplicated effort, conflicting remediations, and significantly extended time-to-resolution
explanation: Communication breakdown in incidents is when responders fail to effectively share information during an active incident, leading to duplicated work, conflicting actions, and slower resolution.

clues: Business teams learn about outages from customer complaints rather than internal channels
The VP of Sales found out about a major service degradation from a client's angry email
Customer support keeps getting blindsided by issues they should have been warned about
The legal team wasn't informed about a data availability issue that triggered SLA breach notification requirements
The enterprise account team discovered a four-hour outage affecting their largest client only after the client's CTO called to complain
The incident management process lacks systematic notification of business stakeholders, causing them to learn about customer-impacting issues from external sources rather than internal communication
explanation: Stakeholder notification failure occurs when the incident management process fails to keep business stakeholders informed, causing them to be blindsided by customer-facing issues.

clues: Customers check the company's service health dashboard and see all green during an active outage
Users flood support channels asking if the service is down because there's no public acknowledgment
The public-facing service status hasn't been updated in weeks despite multiple incidents
During a major outage, the status page showed 'All Systems Operational' for over two hours
The status page component list still references the old product names from before the rebrand and has never reflected the three outages that occurred this quarter
The external status page is systematically neglected during incidents, showing healthy status while customers experience outages, eroding trust and overwhelming support channels
explanation: Status page neglect is the failure to keep public-facing status pages updated during incidents, destroying customer trust and creating unnecessary support burden.

clues: The finance team has started asking engineering about service reliability metrics
Customer contracts contain uptime guarantees but nobody monitors them in real-time
The team realized after the fact that last month's incidents collectively breached a contractual obligation
Enterprise customers are requesting service credit refunds based on measured downtime
The team discovered that three minor incidents in the same month consumed 98% of the quarterly error budget for a tier-1 enterprise SLA
Cumulative service disruptions are approaching or exceeding contractual availability thresholds, exposing the organization to financial penalties that no one is actively tracking
explanation: SLA penalty risk emerges when service disruptions accumulate toward contractual availability thresholds without active monitoring, exposing the organization to financial penalties.

clues: Internal metrics show the system is healthy but customer complaints are rising
The monitoring dashboard is all green yet support tickets about errors have tripled
Engineers can't reproduce issues that customers report consistently
The team's SLIs don't capture the latency customers experience through the CDN and mobile network
Server-side metrics show p99 latency at 200ms but real user monitoring reveals that 15% of mobile users experience over 5 seconds due to unmonitored edge layers
The team's observability stack monitors internal service health but completely misses the customer-facing experience, creating blind spots where users suffer while dashboards show green
explanation: Customer impact blindspot occurs when monitoring and SLIs fail to capture the actual customer experience, creating a false sense of health while users encounter real problems.

clues: An outage in one service unexpectedly cascades to seemingly unrelated services
The team can't answer the question 'what breaks if this component goes down?'
A planned maintenance window caused failures in services nobody expected to be affected
Decommissioning an 'unused' internal API broke three production services that were still calling it
A DNS change for an internal service caused failures in twelve downstream services that weren't listed in any architecture diagram or service registry
The organization lacks a comprehensive map of runtime service dependencies, causing unexpected cascade failures and making it impossible to assess the blast radius of changes
explanation: Dependency mapping gap is the absence of comprehensive knowledge about service interdependencies, leading to unexpected cascading failures and inability to assess change impact.

clues: Nobody trusts the internal directory of services because it's always wrong
Engineers discover services they didn't know existed during incident investigations
The service registry lists an owner for a critical service, but that team was reorganized months ago
A new engineer tried to find the team responsible for the notification service and was sent to three different teams before finding the right one
The internal service catalog lists 47 services but production actually runs 73, with no ownership records for the 26 undocumented ones
The authoritative registry of services, their owners, and their metadata has diverged so far from reality that it actively misleads anyone who consults it
explanation: Service catalog staleness occurs when the internal registry of services and their ownership falls out of date, making it difficult to identify owners, understand dependencies, or respond to incidents.

clues: Incidents that span shift changes take much longer to resolve than those handled within a single shift
The incoming on-call engineer often has no idea what happened during the previous shift
Critical context about an ongoing issue was lost when the on-call rotation changed
An engineer spent two hours re-diagnosing a problem that the previous shift had already root-caused but didn't document
The overnight on-call identified a slow memory leak and applied a temporary mitigation, but left no notes, so the morning shift spent three hours rediscovering the same issue
Critical incident context, investigation progress, and mitigation state are consistently lost at on-call rotation boundaries due to absent or inadequate handoff procedures
explanation: On-call handoff failure is the loss of critical context during shift transitions, causing incoming engineers to repeat work, miss ongoing issues, and extend incident resolution times.

clues: Some engineers dread on-call much more than others on the same team
One person seems to always get paged on weekends while others rarely do
Team morale surveys show on-call burden as the top complaint but only from certain individuals
An analysis reveals that one engineer handled 3x more pages last quarter than any teammate due to how the schedule aligned with peak incident times
The on-call rotation schedule assigns equal calendar time, but due to traffic patterns, the weekend shift handles 80% of all pages while the midweek shift handles almost none
On-call burden is unevenly distributed across rotation participants due to schedule structure, traffic patterns, or unbalanced team composition, leading to burnout for a subset of engineers
explanation: Rotation imbalance occurs when on-call burden is unevenly distributed among team members, often due to schedule structure or traffic patterns, leading to burnout and attrition.

clues: Engineers rarely complete a full day of focused project work
Sprint commitments are consistently missed despite seemingly reasonable scoping
The team's velocity has been declining even though they're working harder than ever
Engineers report context-switching between tickets, pages, and project work more than a dozen times per day
A time study shows the average engineer gets only 90 minutes of uninterrupted project time per day due to constant operational interruptions and ad-hoc requests
Engineering productivity is systematically eroded by a constant stream of operational interrupts, ad-hoc requests, and context switches that prevent sustained focus on planned project work
explanation: Interrupt-driven development occurs when engineers are constantly pulled away from planned work by operational tasks, pages, and ad-hoc requests, destroying focus and reducing project velocity.

clues: The team gets praised for heroic incident response but never for preventing incidents
Engineers who stay up all night fixing outages are celebrated while those who build preventive systems are overlooked
The organization rewards reactive behavior over proactive reliability investment
Promotion packets are full of war stories about saving production, but nobody gets promoted for making production boring
The most senior SRE is beloved for single-handedly resolving dozens of outages, but the team never questions why so many outages happen in the first place
The organization culturally rewards heroic incident response over systemic prevention, creating perverse incentives where outages become resume-building opportunities rather than failures to prevent
explanation: Firefighting culture is when an organization rewards reactive heroics over proactive reliability work, creating incentives that perpetuate the very instability engineers are heroically resolving.

clues: The team has impressive-looking dashboards and processes but reliability isn't actually improving
Management cites the number of postmortems conducted as a reliability success metric
The team performs chaos engineering exercises that are carefully scripted to always succeed
Game days are conducted with pre-planned scenarios that avoid testing any known weak points
The SRE team produces monthly reliability reports showing 99.99% uptime by excluding planned maintenance, partial outages, and any incident shorter than five minutes

The organization invests heavily in the appearance of reliability practices like postmortems, runbooks, and SLOs while ensuring none of these processes produce uncomfortable truths or drive actual change
explanation: Reliability theater is when organizations perform the rituals of reliability engineering without genuinely engaging with the findings, creating an illusion of maturity while actual reliability stagnates.

clues: The system's feature set is sophisticated but operating it feels like early startup chaos
Product development has outpaced the team's ability to reliably operate what they've built
The team ships features weekly but hasn't invested in observability, automation, or incident response processes
A complex microservices architecture is monitored with basic health checks and no distributed tracing
The platform serves millions of users with container orchestration and service mesh, but deployments are manual, there are no SLOs, alerting is threshold-based, and incident response is ad hoc

There is a fundamental mismatch between the sophistication of the technical architecture and the maturity of the operational practices used to run it, creating fragility that grows with each new feature
explanation: Operational maturity gap is the dangerous mismatch between a system's technical complexity and the maturity of the operational practices, tooling, and processes used to run it reliably.

clues: Making a small feature change requires modifying many different files
A simple business rule update touches code in a dozen places
Every time a new payment method is added, five separate modules need identical updates
Adding a new user role required changes in the controller, three services, two middleware layers, and the database schema
Changing the tax calculation logic required edits to 14 files across 6 packages because the tax logic was copied rather than centralized
A single logical change requires coordinated modifications scattered across many modules because the relevant concern was never consolidated into a single cohesive unit
explanation: Shotgun surgery variant is a code smell where a single conceptual change requires many small edits across disparate modules, indicating a missing or fragmented abstraction.

clues: Two unrelated components seem to break together even though they shouldn't be connected
A change in the billing module unexpectedly causes test failures in the notification system
Multiple teams are stepping on each other's toes when modifying shared code
The 'common utilities' package has become a grab bag that every service depends on for different reasons
The shared-models package contains DTOs for orders, users, analytics, and billing, causing all four services to redeploy when any model changes
Unrelated modules share code or data structures not because they have a genuine common abstraction, but out of convenience, creating hidden coupling between independent concerns
explanation: Inappropriate sharing occurs when modules share code or data structures that don't represent genuine commonality, creating hidden coupling between unrelated concerns.

clues: Internal implementation details are visible to consumers of a module
Callers of a class routinely reach into its internal data structures
A library's public API includes methods that only make sense for its internal bookkeeping
The ORM model's internal dirty-tracking fields are accessible to and used by business logic code
The cache implementation exposes its internal HashMap directly, and six different callers manipulate it to implement their own eviction strategies
A module exposes far more of its implementation than necessary in its public interface, allowing and encouraging consumers to depend on internal details that should be encapsulated
explanation: Indecent exposure is a design smell where a module's public interface reveals internal implementation details, allowing consumers to form inappropriate dependencies on internals.

clues: Merge conflicts are a daily occurrence even though people are working on different features
Two developers working on unrelated stories keep editing the same files
Feature branches routinely need multi-hour merge sessions before they can be integrated
The team had to coordinate a 'merge party' because four feature branches all modified the same God class
Two developers adding independent features both had to modify the same 2000-line service class, resulting in a merge conflict touching 200 lines of interleaved changes
Code structure forces developers working on independent features to modify the same files and functions, making parallel development painful and merge-intensive
explanation: Parallel development friction occurs when code structure forces independent workstreams to converge on the same files, causing frequent merge conflicts and coordination overhead.

clues: Two modules work together but there's no explicit interface or contract between them
A change that should be safe breaks a downstream consumer in a surprising way
Services communicate correctly at the data format level but disagree on what the data means
The order service sends status 'completed' meaning 'payment captured,' but the shipping service interprets it as 'ready to ship,' causing premature shipments
Two services pass a 'priority' integer where one uses 1=highest and the other uses 1=lowest, causing critical orders to be processed last
Modules are coupled not through shared types or interfaces but through shared implicit assumptions about the meaning of data, making the coupling invisible to compilers and tests
explanation: Semantic coupling occurs when modules depend on shared implicit meaning rather than explicit contracts, creating invisible dependencies that break when assumptions diverge.

clues: Functions receive large objects but only use a small portion of their data
A method that just needs a user's email address requires a full User entity
Changing an unrelated field on a data structure causes unexpected test failures in far-away code
The notification service accepts the entire 40-field Order object just to read the customer email and order ID
The generateInvoicePdf function takes a full Customer object with 25 fields but only reads name and address, yet it breaks when the unrelated loyalty_points field type changes
Modules pass entire data structures when only a few fields are needed, creating unnecessary dependencies on the structure's complete definition and unrelated fields
explanation: Stamp coupling occurs when modules pass composite data structures containing far more information than needed, creating unnecessary dependencies on unrelated fields.

clues: Functions take boolean parameters that fundamentally change their behavior
The same method does completely different things depending on a flag argument
Callers need to understand a function's internal branching to use it correctly
The process_order(order, is_refund=True) function uses the boolean flag to select between two entirely unrelated code paths
A render(data, mode) function has a mode parameter with 7 possible values, each triggering a different internal algorithm that shares almost no code
One module controls another's internal behavior by passing flags or control parameters, forcing callers to understand the callee's internal logic to use it correctly
explanation: Control coupling occurs when one module passes control flags that dictate another module's internal execution path, revealing and depending on the callee's internal logic.

clues: Modules exchange data through shared global state rather than explicit parameters
Functions produce results by modifying external variables instead of returning values
Debugging is difficult because data flows through hidden channels rather than method signatures
Two classes communicate by writing to and reading from a shared singleton that acts as a hidden message bus
The pricing engine reads its inputs from a ThreadLocal variable set by the controller, and writes its output to a global cache that the checkout service polls
Modules exchange information through implicit shared state rather than explicit parameters and return values, making data flow invisible and the system impossible to reason about locally
explanation: Data coupling violation occurs when modules communicate through implicit shared state instead of explicit parameters and return values, obscuring data flow and making the system hard to understand.

clues: Multiple modules depend on the same global data, and changes to it affect all of them
A configuration change causes unexpected failures in seemingly unrelated features
Race conditions appear because several components read and write the same shared resource
Five different services all read and write to the same global configuration table, and updating one setting for billing broke the email service
Twelve modules share a global AppState object, and adding a field for the new reporting feature caused memory layout changes that broke serialization in the auth module
Multiple modules are coupled through shared access to global data, meaning any module can affect all others by modifying the shared state, creating unpredictable interactions
explanation: Common coupling occurs when multiple modules share access to global data, creating hidden interdependencies where any module's modification of the shared data can break all the others.

clues: One module directly accesses or modifies the internal data of another module
Code reaches into another class's private fields using reflection or memory manipulation
A performance optimization bypasses a module's public API to directly manipulate its internal state
The caching layer directly modifies the ORM entity's internal __dirty_fields list to force a database write
Module A uses reflection to set Module B's private _connectionPool field to null during testing, and production code similarly reaches in to adjust the pool size
One module depends on and directly manipulates the internal implementation details of another, creating the tightest possible coupling where any internal change breaks the dependent module
explanation: Content coupling is the tightest form of coupling, where one module directly accesses or modifies another module's internal implementation, making any internal change a breaking change.

clues: Code works fine in development but fails intermittently in production
Tests pass when run individually but fail when run in a different order
A refactoring that reordered two method calls caused a subtle production bug
The initialize() method must be called before process() or the system silently produces wrong results, but nothing enforces this ordering
The system crashes if the cache warming function runs after the request handler starts, but this ordering dependency is only documented in a code comment from 2019
Methods or components must be invoked in a specific order to function correctly, but this ordering requirement is implicit rather than enforced by the type system or API design
explanation: Temporal coupling occurs when code correctness depends on operations being performed in a specific order, but this ordering constraint is implicit rather than enforced by the design.

clues: An object's methods must be called in a specific sequence or it enters an invalid state
Users of a class frequently forget to call a required setup method before using the main functionality
A code review comment says 'make sure you call connect() before send() or it will throw a NullPointerException'
The Builder class requires setHost(), then setPort(), then setCredentials() in exact order, and calling them out of sequence produces a cryptic error
The EmailClient class requires open(), authenticate(), setRecipient(), setBody(), send(), close() called in exact sequence, and skipping or reordering any step causes silent data corruption
A class requires its methods to be called in a rigid sequence where each step depends on side effects from the previous one, but the API doesn't prevent or guide correct ordering
explanation: Sequential coupling is when a class's methods must be called in a specific sequence to function correctly, but the API design doesn't enforce or guide the correct calling order.

clues: The codebase reads like a procedural program wrapped in classes
Each class has exactly one method that does all the work
The object model mirrors the steps of a process rather than the domain concepts
Classes are named things like 'Step1Validator', 'Step2Enricher', 'Step3Persister' with no domain meaning
The system has classes named InputReader, DataValidator, BusinessRuleApplier, OutputFormatter, and DatabaseWriter, each with a single execute() method and no state
The design decomposes the system along procedural steps rather than domain concepts, producing classes that are just functions in disguise with no meaningful object-oriented structure
explanation: Functional decomposition failure occurs when a system is broken down into procedural steps rather than domain objects, resulting in classes that are thin wrappers around single functions.

clues: Objects freely access each other's internal state without encapsulation
Most fields are public and every class reaches into every other class's data
There are no meaningful access boundaries between components in the system
Any class can modify any other class's state at any time, making it impossible to determine which code changed a field
The Order, Customer, and Inventory classes all have public fields and directly modify each other's internal data structures without going through methods
The codebase has near-zero encapsulation, with objects freely reading and modifying each other's internal state, making it impossible to reason about invariants or data ownership
explanation: Object orgy is an antipattern where objects lack encapsulation and freely access each other's internals, destroying any ability to maintain invariants or reason about data ownership.

clues: Some classes seem to exist only to instantiate or invoke other classes
A code review reveals classes with no real responsibility that just pass-through to other objects
Removing a class and having callers talk directly to the next class would simplify the code with no loss of functionality
The OrderManager class's only method creates an OrderProcessor and calls process() on it, adding no logic of its own
The SystemInitializer class exists solely to instantiate ServiceLocator, which instantiates ServiceFactory, which creates the actual service -- each intermediary adds zero logic
Short-lived classes exist only to briefly appear, invoke another class, and disappear, adding no behavior, state, or abstraction -- merely inserting an unnecessary layer of indirection
explanation: The poltergeist pattern is an antipattern where classes appear briefly to invoke other classes and then vanish, adding indirection without adding value.

clues: Methods are chained so deeply that a single expression spans multiple lines
A NullPointerException in production points to a long chain of method calls with no indication which link failed
Code looks like a chain of dots connecting many objects together in a single expression
The expression order.getCustomer().getAddress().getCity().getZipCode().substring(0,3) appears throughout the codebase
A single line reads ctx.getSession().getUser().getProfile().getPreferences().getNotificationSettings().getEmailFrequency() and any null in the chain causes an undiagnosable NPE
Long chains of method calls violate the Law of Demeter by reaching through multiple layers of objects, creating fragile code that breaks when any intermediate object's structure changes
explanation: Train wreck code is deeply chained method calls that violate the Law of Demeter, creating fragile chains where any intermediate change breaks callers and null values are impossible to diagnose.

clues: Code indentation keeps going deeper and deeper to the right
Functions have 5 or more levels of nesting that make them hard to follow
When you look at the code sideways, the indentation forms a distinctive pointed shape
A function has if/else blocks nested eight levels deep, with the core logic buried at the innermost level requiring horizontal scrolling to read
The processOrder function nests validation inside authorization inside inventory checks inside pricing inside tax calculation, forming a right-pointing arrow shape that reaches column 120
Deeply nested conditional logic creates code whose indentation profile resembles a rightward-pointing arrow, with the meaningful business logic buried at the innermost nesting level
explanation: The arrow antipattern is deeply nested conditional code whose indentation forms an arrow shape, burying core logic at extreme indentation levels and making the code difficult to follow.

clues: Asynchronous code has become deeply nested and hard to reason about
Each async operation is initiated inside the callback of the previous one
Error handling is duplicated at every nesting level of the callback chain
The code has five levels of nested callbacks for a sequence of database query, API call, file write, notification send, and audit log
A Node.js function nests readFile(callback(writeDB(callback(sendEmail(callback(logAudit(callback(updateCache(callback))))))))) with error handling duplicated at each level
Deeply nested callback-based async code forms a rightward-drifting pyramid shape, with each asynchronous step adding another level of indentation and duplicated error handling
explanation: Pyramid of doom is the pattern of deeply nested callbacks in asynchronous code, where each async step adds indentation and error handling, making the code unreadable and unmaintainable.

clues: Reading a function requires mentally tracking many branching paths simultaneously
The cyclomatic complexity of a single function is over 30
Adding a new business rule requires understanding every possible path through a deeply nested function
A single function has 12 if/else branches and every new condition is added inside an existing branch rather than refactored
The applyDiscount function checks user type, then membership tier, then region, then order size, then day of week, each nested inside the previous, with 47 distinct code paths

Business logic is expressed as deeply nested conditional branches where each new requirement adds another layer of nesting, creating combinatorial complexity that makes the function impossible to test or modify safely
explanation: Nested conditional hell is when business logic accumulates as deeply nested if/else branches, creating combinatorial complexity that makes the code untestable and dangerous to modify.

clues: Functions have deeply nested happy paths because edge cases aren't handled up front
The main logic of a method is indented several levels because of precondition checks wrapping it
Code reviewers keep suggesting 'just return early' on pull requests
A function wraps its entire body in 'if (input != null)' instead of returning early when input is null
The processPayment method nests the entire 60-line business logic inside if(user != null) { if(order != null) { if(order.total > 0) { ... } } } instead of validating and returning early
Functions fail to validate preconditions and return early at the top, instead wrapping the entire function body in nested conditional checks that increase indentation and obscure the happy path
explanation: Guard clause absence is when functions lack early returns for precondition failures, forcing the entire function body into nested conditionals instead of failing fast at the top.

clues: Functions always have a single return statement at the very end, even when it makes the code more complex
Temporary variables are used to hold results through long conditional chains just to return once at the bottom
Code reviews reveal a team norm against having multiple return statements in a function
A 100-line function uses a 'result' variable set in different branches throughout, with a single return result at the end, when 5 early returns would halve the complexity
The team's coding standard mandates single-exit functions, causing a 200-line method to use nested flags and a 'returnValue' variable threaded through 8 branches instead of 6 clear early returns

A coding convention prohibiting multiple return statements forces unnecessarily complex control flow, using flags and result variables to simulate the clarity that early returns would naturally provide
explanation: Early return resistance is a convention that prohibits multiple return statements, forcing complex control flow with flags and result variables where early returns would be cleaner.

clues: Exceptions are used for normal program control flow rather than exceptional situations
Try-catch blocks appear around ordinary business logic, not just error-prone operations
Performance profiling reveals that exception creation is a significant CPU cost
The code uses a NotFoundException to signal 'user has no orders' as a normal business case, throwing and catching it hundreds of times per second
The authentication flow uses throw new NotLoggedInException() as the standard way to redirect to the login page, generating millions of stack traces per day for normal user behavior
Exceptions are systematically used as a control flow mechanism for expected business conditions, incurring stack trace overhead and obscuring genuinely exceptional error cases
explanation: Exception handling abuse is the misuse of exceptions for normal control flow rather than truly exceptional situations, degrading performance and obscuring real errors.

clues: Functions return magic numbers to indicate different error conditions
Callers must check return values against a table of numeric codes after every function call
Error handling is easy to forget because nothing forces callers to check the return value
Functions return 0 for success, -1 for not found, -2 for permission denied, -3 for timeout, and callers frequently forget to check, silently treating errors as success
The entire API layer returns integer error codes where -1 means 12 different things depending on the function, and a production bug was caused by a caller ignoring a -3 return value

The system relies on numeric return codes instead of exceptions or result types to communicate errors, making it easy to ignore failures and impossible to distinguish error conditions without consulting documentation
explanation: Error code overuse is excessive reliance on numeric return codes for error signaling, which callers can easily ignore and which convey less information than exceptions or result types.

clues: The codebase is littered with null checks before every method call
NullPointerExceptions are the most common production error type
Every consumer of an interface must handle the 'not found' case with explicit null checking
Dozens of methods start with 'if (user.getPreferences() != null)' before accessing any preference, with different fallback logic in each place
The getDiscount() method is called from 30 places, and each one wraps it in a null check with a default of zero, when a NullDiscount object returning zero would eliminate all 30 checks
The absence of null object implementations forces every call site to perform defensive null checks, scattering default behavior across the codebase instead of encapsulating it in a single place
explanation: Null object pattern absence is when the codebase lacks null object implementations, forcing pervasive null checking at every call site instead of encapsulating default behavior.

clues: Domain concepts are represented as raw strings and integers throughout the codebase
Validation logic for the same concept is duplicated wherever that value is used
A bug was caused by passing a customer ID where an order ID was expected, and the compiler didn't catch it because both are strings
Email addresses are plain strings validated in 14 different places with 14 slightly different regex patterns

The function transfer(amount: number, from: string, to: string) accepts any number for amount and any string for account IDs, and a production bug swapped from/to because the compiler couldn't distinguish them

Domain concepts like money, email, and identifiers are represented as primitive types rather than value objects, losing type safety, scattering validation, and enabling subtle bugs from type confusion
explanation: Primitive obsession variant is the pervasive use of primitive types for domain concepts, losing type safety and scattering validation logic that should be encapsulated in value objects.

clues: Building one service takes much longer than expected for its size
A code change in one service triggers CI builds for unrelated services
Developers complain that their test suite runs code from packages they never modified
Merging a one-line change to the payments service triggers a 45-minute build that compiles and tests all 12 services in the repository
The CI pipeline builds every service on every commit because the build system can't determine dependency boundaries, causing 3-hour build queues from a monorepo with 20 services
In a monorepo, the build system fails to scope builds to affected services, causing every change to trigger compilation and testing of unrelated services, wasting CI resources and developer time
explanation: Monorepo build contamination occurs when a monorepo's build system cannot scope builds to affected services, causing changes to one service to trigger builds across the entire repository.

clues: Releasing a feature requires synchronized deployments across multiple repositories
Teams frequently break each other's services because API changes aren't coordinated
A feature that spans two services takes three times longer than one that fits in a single service
The frontend and backend repos must be deployed within minutes of each other or users see broken pages, but there's no coordination mechanism
A three-service feature required 12 pull requests across 4 repositories with manual coordination in a spreadsheet, and the release failed twice due to ordering mistakes
Distributing related code across separate repositories without coordination mechanisms makes cross-cutting changes painful, error-prone, and requires manual synchronization of releases
explanation: Polyrepo coordination failure occurs when related services split across repositories lack coordination mechanisms, making cross-cutting changes and synchronized releases painful and error-prone.

clues: Different services behave inconsistently even though they use the same shared library
A bug fix in a common library only takes effect in some services because others haven't updated
The security team's patch for the authentication library is only deployed in 3 of 8 services that use it
Service A uses shared-utils v1.2 with a critical bug fix while services B, C, and D still run v1.0 with the vulnerability
The internal HTTP client library has 7 different versions in production across 15 services, with the oldest version missing two years of security patches
Services depend on different versions of a shared internal library, causing inconsistent behavior, fragmented security patches, and subtle bugs that only manifest in services running older versions
explanation: Shared library version skew occurs when services use different versions of a shared library, causing inconsistent behavior and making it difficult to roll out fixes uniformly.

clues: Adding a new library causes mysterious build failures in unrelated parts of the application
Two direct dependencies each require different versions of the same underlying library
The application works in development but fails in production due to a different version of a nested dependency being resolved
Library A requires jackson 2.13 and library B requires jackson 2.10, causing ClassNotFoundException at runtime depending on which version the build resolves
The payment SDK requires protobuf 3.19 and the messaging SDK requires protobuf 3.12, and Maven's nearest-wins resolution silently picks one, causing runtime serialization failures in the other
Two or more direct dependencies each require incompatible versions of the same transitive dependency, and the build system's resolution strategy silently picks one, causing runtime failures
explanation: Transitive dependency conflicts occur when direct dependencies require incompatible versions of the same transitive dependency, and the build resolver silently picks one, causing runtime failures.

clues: A module upgrade causes failures in seemingly unrelated parts of the dependency tree
Two intermediate libraries both depend on the same base library but require different versions
The dependency graph has a diamond shape where paths converge on a single library with version conflicts
Service S depends on libraries A and B, which both depend on library C but at incompatible versions, and only one version of C can be loaded
The app depends on auth-lib and payment-lib, both of which depend on crypto-utils. Upgrading auth-lib to use crypto-utils v3 breaks payment-lib which requires crypto-utils v2 APIs
A diamond-shaped dependency graph creates a conflict where two intermediate dependencies require incompatible versions of a common base dependency, and the runtime can only load one
explanation: The diamond dependency problem is a specific dependency conflict where two intermediate dependencies both require different versions of the same base dependency, creating an irreconcilable conflict.

clues: The project's dependencies haven't been updated in over a year
Security scanners keep flagging known vulnerabilities in third-party libraries
Upgrading one dependency now requires upgrading ten others because they've all drifted so far behind
The team pinned all dependencies to exact versions for stability two years ago and hasn't touched them since, accumulating 47 known CVEs

Every dependency in package.json is pinned to an exact version from 2023, and attempting to upgrade React triggers a cascade of 30 peer dependency conflicts because nothing has been updated incrementally

Dependencies pinned to exact versions for stability have become stale over time, accumulating security vulnerabilities and making eventual upgrades exponentially harder due to compounding version drift
explanation: Pinned dependency staleness occurs when exact-version pinning prevents incremental updates, causing dependencies to age until upgrades become massive, risky undertakings.

clues: Builds sometimes fail for no apparent code change
The same commit produces different artifacts when built at different times
A production incident was traced to a patch-version bump in a transitive dependency that happened between the staging and production builds
The build broke on Monday because a dependency specified as ^2.0.0 auto-resolved to 2.3.0 which had a breaking change despite being a minor version bump
Using semver ranges like ~1.2.0 in package.json meant that the Tuesday build pulled a different patch version than Monday's, introducing a subtle regression that only appeared in production
Dependencies specified with semver ranges automatically resolve to newer versions between builds, creating non-reproducible builds where the same source code produces different artifacts over time
explanation: Floating dependency risk is when semver range specifiers allow automatic resolution to newer versions between builds, breaking reproducibility and introducing unexpected changes.

clues: Different developers get different build results from the same branch
The CI build succeeds but a developer's local build fails, or vice versa
A critical file that ensures reproducible dependency resolution is excluded from version control
The .gitignore includes package-lock.json, so every developer and CI run resolves dependencies independently, producing different node_modules trees
The team added yarn.lock to .gitignore because merge conflicts in it were annoying, causing 'works on my machine' issues when developers resolve different dependency versions
The dependency lock file is missing from version control or not consistently used, causing different environments to resolve different dependency versions from the same specification
explanation: Lock file neglect is the failure to commit and maintain dependency lock files in version control, causing different environments to resolve different dependency versions.

clues: The application works on developers' machines but fails in the CI environment
Docker builds fail with compilation errors that don't occur locally
A dependency requires a C compiler and system libraries that aren't available in every environment
The bcrypt npm package fails to install in the Alpine Linux container because it needs glibc but Alpine uses musl
The Python image processing library requires libffi, libjpeg, and zlib headers to compile its C extension, causing installation failures on minimal container base images
Dependencies with native compiled extensions fail to build or run across different operating systems and architectures because they require platform-specific compilers, headers, and system libraries
explanation: Native extension portability issues arise when dependencies with compiled native code require platform-specific build tools and libraries that aren't available in all target environments.

clues: The application works on macOS but fails on Linux, or vice versa
Tests pass locally but fail in CI with path-related or filesystem errors
