explanation: Premature microservices is the antipattern of adopting a microservice architecture before the team size, domain understanding, or scale justifies the operational complexity.

clues: A bad deploy made it to 100% of production even though we have a canary process
Our gradual rollout did not detect the regression that customers immediately noticed
The canary received traffic but the metrics looked healthy throughout the entire rollout
The bug only manifests for a specific user segment that was not represented in canary traffic
Our canary checks latency and error rate but the regression was a subtle data correctness issue not covered by those metrics
The canary deployment only monitors p99 latency and 5xx rate, so a bug that silently returns wrong data to 10% of users passed canary analysis and rolled out fully
explanation: A canary that doesn't catch is a deployment canary that fails to detect regressions because it monitors the wrong metrics or doesn't receive representative traffic.

clues: Our cloud bill keeps growing even though we have not launched any new services recently
There are resources running in our account that nobody on the current team recognizes
These resources were created for a project that was cancelled months ago but never cleaned up
The Terraform state file references resources that no longer match any active codebase
EC2 instances, RDS databases, and S3 buckets sit idle with no service routing traffic to them
A load balancer, three EC2 instances, and an RDS cluster from a decommissioned project are still running, costing $2,000/month with no active service using them
explanation: Orphaned infrastructure refers to cloud resources left running after their associated project or service has been decommissioned, wasting money and creating security risk.

clues: Our API surface area keeps growing but nobody is sure which endpoints are still in use
Documentation references endpoints that may or may not still work correctly
Access logs show some endpoints have received zero traffic in months
Nobody wants to remove old endpoints for fear of breaking unknown consumers
Deprecated API routes still exist in the codebase with no known callers, but no one dares delete them
The /v1/legacy-sync endpoint was deprecated two years ago and gets zero requests, but it still has database access and an active route, creating maintenance burden and security surface
explanation: Zombie endpoints are API routes that are no longer actively used but remain deployed, creating maintenance burden, security risk, and API surface bloat.

clues: Users report seeing outdated information even after refreshing the page
The data in our database is correct, but the application keeps serving old values
The issue affects specific data that was recently updated, not all data
Our cache TTL is set to 24 hours, but the underlying data can change every few minutes
A user updates their profile, but the old profile keeps being served to everyone because the cache has not expired yet
A product price was updated from $10 to $15 in the database, but the CDN and application cache continue serving $10 until the 24-hour TTL expires
explanation: A stale cache serves outdated data because the cache entry has not been refreshed or invalidated after the source data changed.

clues: Every bug fix in a certain area of the codebase seems to introduce a new bug somewhere else
Code reviews for changes in this file take forever because the reviewer cannot understand all the side effects
One method in the codebase handles validation, business logic, persistence, notification, and logging all in one place
The method is over 500 lines long with deeply nested conditionals and multiple responsibilities
Adding a new feature requires modifying this single enormous method because all the business logic lives there
A single processOrder() method handles input validation, inventory checks, payment processing, shipping calculations, email notifications, and audit logging in 800 lines of tangled code
explanation: A god method is an excessively large method that handles too many responsibilities, making it difficult to understand, test, and modify safely.

clues: Our codebase has a lot of conditional branches that make the logic hard to follow
Code coverage reports show branches that are never exercised in any environment
Feature flags that were fully rolled out months ago still have if/else branches in the code
The flag management dashboard shows flags marked as 100% enabled for all users since last year
Developers are afraid to remove the old code path because they are not sure the flag will never be toggled back
A feature flag launched 18 months ago is permanently enabled, but both the old and new code paths remain, with the dead else branch adding complexity and confusing new team members
explanation: A feature flag stale branch occurs when a feature flag has been fully rolled out but its conditional branches are never cleaned up, leaving dead code in the codebase.

clues: Our on-call engineers are overwhelmed with alerts but struggle to figure out what is actually wrong
Multiple dashboards light up red simultaneously, making it hard to find the root issue
We get paged for high CPU, high memory, and high latency, which are all effects of one underlying problem
Engineers spend the first 30 minutes of every incident correlating alerts to find the real cause
All our alerts fire on resource metrics and error rates, but none point to the specific component or condition that caused the problem
An alert fires for high p99 latency, another for increased 5xx errors, and another for CPU spike, but none indicate that the root cause is a full connection pool in the payment service
explanation: Alerting on symptoms vs causes means monitoring surface-level metrics like CPU or error rates instead of the underlying conditions that produce them, making incident diagnosis slow.
